proposals.title: Enhancing future-oriented decision-making in older adults through hippocampal neurofeedback: A real-time fMRI study of episodic simulation and temporal discounting
proposals.motivation: Age-related decline in episodic simulation, linked to hippocampal function, impairs the ability to vividly imagine future outcomes, a process known as the 'episodic tag-effect'. This impairment is associated with steeper temporal discounting (preferring smaller immediate rewards over larger delayed ones) and poorer long-term decision-making in older adults. Pre-2026, real-time fMRI neurofeedback (rtfMRI-NF) has shown promise for modulating brain regions like the amygdala, but its application to the hippocampus for enhancing cognitive functions related to future thinking remains underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that real-time fMRI neurofeedback training to increase hippocampal activation during episodic simulation of future rewards will enhance the episodic tag-effect, leading to reduced temporal discounting rates (i.e., more future-oriented choices) in healthy older adults.
proposals.method: We will recruit older adult participants (65+ years) to complete a temporal discounting task with an embedded episodic simulation component inside the MRI scanner. Using a block-design rtfMRI-NF paradigm, participants will receive visual feedback (e.g., a thermometer display) based on the level of BOLD activation in a pre-defined hippocampal region of interest (ROI), derived from a functional localizer. The feedback algorithm will utilize a general linear model (GLM) implemented in Turbo-BrainVoyager or the OpenNFT toolbox for real-time analysis. Participants will be trained to up-regulate hippocampal activity by vividly simulating personal future events linked to delayed monetary rewards presented in the choice task.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first causal evidence that targeted hippocampal neurofeedback can directly modify a core cognitive mechanism (episodic simulation) underlying intertemporal choice. It will contribute to the development of non-pharmacological, neuroscience-based interventions for improving financial and health decision-making in aging populations. Furthermore, it will establish a novel methodological bridge between neurofeedback research and the behavioral economics of discounting.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Peters and others has studied the reliability of discounting measures and the neural correlates of the tag-effect, actively training the hippocampus via neurofeedback to improve this effect is a novel, causal intervention. The integration of a well-established behavioral economic paradigm (temporal discounting) with a cutting-edge neuromodulation technique (rtfMRI-NF) for cognitive enhancement in aging is highly innovative.

proposals.title: Episodic Future Tagging and Temporal Discounting: A Causal Test of Aversive Prospective Memory on Impulsive Choice
proposals.motivation: Temporal discounting is a core process in decision-making, linked to disorders like addiction and gambling. While prior work shows episodic future thinking (EFT) can reduce discounting by making the future vivid, the inverse mechanism—where negative episodic tags *increase* discounting—remains untested. This gap is critical because real-world future events are often uncertain or aversive, potentially exacerbating impulsivity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that experimentally inducing negative, episodic future tags (vs. neutral or positive tags) will increase discounting rates by making the delayed future seem more aversive and uncertain, thereby elevating the subjective value of immediate rewards.
proposals.method: We will recruit 120 participants for a within-subjects virtual reality (VR) study. Using a validated VR temporal discounting task, we will measure discounting rates (k) under three conditions: after immersive VR scenarios designed to tag a future timepoint with negative, neutral, or positive episodic content. Reactivity of discounting will be assessed via trial-by-trial computational modeling using hierarchical Bayesian estimation of hyperbolic discount functions. We will also measure associated state anxiety and subjective certainty about the future.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first causal evidence for a maladaptive, inverse effect of episodic tagging on choice, moving beyond EFT's therapeutic framing. It will identify a novel cognitive mechanism (aversive prospective memory) that may contribute to pathological impulsivity, offering a new target for clinical intervention.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the established EFT/discounting paradigm to test a deleterious effect, and integrates VR-based episodic tagging with computational psychiatry methods to model trial-level decision dynamics, a novel synthesis of Peters' recent methodological themes.

proposals.title: Disentangling Trait and Context: A Longitudinal Ecological Momentary Assessment of Temporal Discounting Stability
proposals.motivation: Temporal discounting is widely treated as a stable trait linked to disorders like addiction and ADHD, but its measurement reliability is debated. Pre-2026 studies, including Peters' own work on reliability in VR, suggest environmental constancy in control groups may artificially inflate stability estimates, conflating true trait-like properties with contextual invariance. Resolving this is critical for interpreting discounting as a clinical biomarker or a target for intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that apparent temporal discounting stability in healthy controls is primarily a function of stable environmental contexts, and that experimentally manipulating context across time will reveal significant, predictable within-person variability, challenging its status as a pure trait.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 6-month longitudinal study with 150 participants, combining lab-based assessments with Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA). Participants will complete adaptive, trial-based discounting tasks weekly in a controlled VR environment (leveraging Peters' prior method) and daily via a smartphone app presenting brief, validated choice items. Contextual variables (stress, location, social setting) will be sampled via EMA. We will use Hierarchical Bayesian Drift-Diffusion Modeling (DDM) to decompose choices into latent decision components (drift rate, threshold) and multilevel models to partition variance into within-person (state) and between-person (trait) components, testing reactivity to context.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first direct estimate of the proportion of temporal discounting variance attributable to stable trait vs. contextual state factors. It will establish a robust methodological framework for assessing decision-making trait stability and clarify the conditions under which discounting can be reliably used as a clinical or individual differences measure.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has tested reliability or context effects in isolation, this proposal uniquely combines longitudinal EMA, VR assessment, and computational modeling to directly pit trait versus state explanations, moving beyond simple test-retest correlations to a mechanistic variance partitioning model.

proposals.title: Deep Brain Stimulation Modulates Neural Drift and Preference Stability: A Combined fMRI and Computational Modeling Study
proposals.motivation: Deep Brain Stimulation (DBS) for movement disorders like Parkinson's disease can induce profound changes in decision-making, including increased impulsivity and gambling, which are core to Peters' research themes. These behavioral shifts suggest a disruption of stable value representations, but the circuit-level neural mechanisms—particularly how DBS alters the temporal dynamics of choice—remain poorly understood. Elucidating these mechanisms is critical for developing next-generation DBS protocols that mitigate adverse neuropsychiatric side-effects.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that DBS of the subthalamic nucleus disrupts the stability of value representations in medial temporal lobe (MTL) and ventromedial prefrontal cortex (vmPFC) circuits, and that this disruption is mechanistically explained by an increase in trial-to-trial neural 'drift' in these regions during preference judgments.
proposals.method: We will recruit Parkinson's patients with implanted DBS systems undergoing clinical programming. During fMRI scanning, participants will perform a well-validated temporal discounting and gambling task across DBS-ON and DBS-OFF blocks in a counterbalanced design. We will use multi-echo fMRI to improve signal in subcortical regions. Neural data will be analyzed using a combination of multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) in MTL subregions (informed by Peters' prior work) to track value code reinstatement, and a hierarchical Bayesian implementation of the drift-diffusion model (DDM) to link trial-wise BOLD signal variability in vmPFC and striatum to parameters of choice drift and reactivity.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct evidence of how DBS modulates the neural dynamics of economic preference, moving beyond behavioral correlations to a circuit-based explanation. It will establish a novel neurocomputational framework linking DBS, neural variability, and choice instability, with direct relevance for understanding DBS-related impulse control disorders.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies associate DBS with behavioral changes, this proposal uniquely combines acute DBS manipulation with computational modeling of neural drift to identify a mechanistic pathway for preference disruption, directly integrating Peters' expertise in discounting, temporal dynamics, and reliability assessment.

proposals.title: Causal control of temporal preference stability: Nucleus accumbens deep brain stimulation in a rodent discounting paradigm
proposals.motivation: Human studies show temporal discounting is a trans-diagnostic marker for disorders like addiction and impulsivity, but its neural mechanisms remain correlational. Deep brain stimulation (DBS) of the nucleus accumbens (NAc) is a clinical intervention for obsessive-compulsive disorder, yet its precise effects on dynamic decision processes like intertemporal choice are poorly understood. An animal model allowing causal, mechanistic manipulation is needed to bridge this gap and inform targeted neuromodulation therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that high-frequency DBS of the rodent NAc core will increase preference stability (reduce choice variability) for delayed rewards without systematically altering the discount rate itself, by modulating neural noise or drift processes associated with value comparison.
proposals.method: Rats will be trained on a dynamic, within-session adjusting-delay discounting task in operant chambers, generating a psychometric curve of choice probability vs. delay difference. Following training, animals will be implanted with bilateral DBS electrodes targeting the NAc core. Using a within-subjects design, we will administer high-frequency (130 Hz) vs. sham stimulation during choice blocks. Choice behavior will be modeled using a Hierarchical Drift-Diffusion Model (HDDM) framework to decompose effects on discount rate (drift rate) from effects on decision noise/variability (boundary separation and non-decision time). Neural reactivity will be assessed via immediate early gene expression (c-Fos) in downstream targets post-session.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a causal link between NAc activity patterns and the computational components of intertemporal choice, specifically isolating a circuit mechanism for preference stability. It will provide a preclinical model for testing DBS parameters on decision dynamics, directly informing clinical applications for disorders of behavioral control. The study will also validate the use of virtual-reality-inspired dynamic tasks in rodents for assessing reliability of discounting measures.
proposals.novelty: While rodent discounting models exist, none have combined real-time NAc DBS with trial-by-trial computational modeling to dissect its effects on specific decision parameters (drift vs. boundary). This moves beyond simply measuring changes in discount rate to target the neural basis of choice consistency, a novel focus given prior DBS work on reward magnitude or motivation.

proposals.title: Dissociating Neural Representations of Subjective Value and Reward Uncertainty in the Nucleus Accumbens During Temporal Discounting
proposals.motivation: A core finding in neuroeconomics is that nucleus accumbens (NAcc) BOLD signal scales with the subjective value of delayed rewards. However, this signal is also attenuated for uncertain rewards, creating a critical confound: is reduced NAcc activity due to lower subjective value or to the separate encoding of outcome uncertainty? Resolving this ambiguity is essential for accurate models of reward valuation, with implications for understanding disorders like addiction and pathological gambling where both delay and uncertainty discounting are altered.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that attenuated NAcc activation during choices involving probabilistic rewards reflects the encoding of reward uncertainty per se, rather than a diminished representation of the option's subjective value. This neural signature of uncertainty will be dissociable from the value signal observed during standard temporal discounting.
proposals.method: We will recruit 60 participants to complete a modified delay discounting task in a virtual reality environment (enhancing ecological validity and reliability) where rewards are delayed, probabilistic, or both. Functional MRI data will be acquired during task performance. We will use a hierarchical drift-diffusion model (HDDM) to separately estimate trial-wise subjective value and choice uncertainty parameters from behavior. These trial-by-trial regressors will be used in a general linear model to identify NAcc voxels where BOLD signal correlates uniquely with (a) modeled subjective value and (b) residual uncertainty, after controlling for the other.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a direct test of a competing account for a fundamental neuroeconomic signal, clarifying the computational role of the NAcc. It will yield a refined neural model for value-based choice that parses value and uncertainty. The findings will inform theories of impulsivity and risk-taking, potentially identifying distinct neural markers for temporal and probabilistic discounting dysregulation.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have linked NAcc to both value and uncertainty, this design explicitly orthogonalizes these variables within a discounting framework. Combining computational modeling (HDDM) of choice dynamics with fMRI in an immersive VR task allows us to isolate the unique neural correlates of uncertainty from diminished value with greater precision than previous correlational approaches.

proposals.title: Directed Connectivity in the Valuation Network: A Dynamic Causal Modeling Study of Domain-General and Domain-Specific Interactions During Intertemporal and Risky Choice
proposals.motivation: Neuroeconomic research pre-2026 has identified a core valuation network, including the ventromedial prefrontal cortex (vmPFC) as a domain-general integrator and regions like the ventral striatum and amygdala as more domain-specific for reward and risk. However, the directed, causal interactions between these regions during different types of value-based choices (e.g., temporal discounting vs. gambling) remain poorly understood. This limits our ability to model the neural mechanisms underlying individual differences in impulsivity and risk-taking, which are associated with various disorders.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that during intertemporal choice, effective connectivity from the ventral striatum to the vmPFC will be stronger, whereas during risky choice, the amygdala will exert a stronger driving influence on vmPFC activity. Furthermore, we predict that individual differences in discount rates and risk preferences will be correlated with the strength of these directed connections.
proposals.method: We will collect fMRI data from 50 participants performing well-established intertemporal and risky choice tasks. Using SPM12, we will implement a Dynamic Causal Modeling (DCM) analysis. We will construct a biologically plausible network model with nodes in vmPFC, ventral striatum, and amygdala. We will compare families of DCMs representing different directed connectivity architectures (e.g., driving vs. modulatory inputs) to identify the most likely model of information flow for each task. Parameter estimates from the winning model will be correlated with behavioral measures of discounting (k) and risk sensitivity (α).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first formal, model-based evidence for directed causal influences within the human valuation network, distinguishing between choice domains. It will advance neuroeconomic theory beyond mere co-activation to a mechanistic understanding of network dynamics. The findings could inform future studies on the neural basis of dysfunctional decision-making in disorders characterized by impulsivity or altered risk perception.
proposals.novelty: While DCM has been used in systems neuroscience, its application to dissect the directed connectivity of the valuation network during distinct, ecologically valid choice tasks is novel. This moves beyond the standard general linear model approaches used in most pre-2026 neuroeconomics to test specific hypotheses about information flow.

proposals.title: Dissociating Anticipatory and Consummatory Neural Dynamics in Reward Processing: A Drift-Diffusion and Reactivity Study of Gambling Vulnerability
proposals.motivation: Current models of reward dysfunction, particularly in gambling and substance use disorders, often conflate neural and behavioral responses across reward phases. This limits our understanding of vulnerability, as distinct profiles (e.g., blunted anticipation vs. exaggerated consumption) may have different clinical implications. Grounded in pre-2026 work on temporal discounting and reward reactivity, this study aims to formally dissociate these phases to refine predictive models of maladaptive choice.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a specific vulnerability profile for impulsive choice is characterized by hypo-reactivity in the striatum during reward anticipation, coupled with hyper-reactivity in the ventral striatum and vmPFC during reward consumption. This dissociated profile will be more strongly associated with steep temporal discounting and gambling severity than global reward hyper- or hypo-responsiveness.
proposals.method: We will recruit participants with varying levels of gambling severity and healthy controls. Using a modified Monetary Incentive Delay (MID) task in an fMRI environment, we will isolate BOLD signal during anticipation and consumption phases. Behavioral choice data from a concurrent temporal discounting task will be modeled using the drift-diffusion model (DDM) to quantify evidence accumulation rates and decision thresholds. We will then use multi-voxel pattern analysis (MVPA) in medial temporal lobe and striatal subregions to test for phase-specific neural patterns and correlate these with DDM parameters and clinical measures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a more nuanced, phase-specific neural and computational account of reward dysfunction, moving beyond unitary constructs of 'reward sensitivity'. It will establish a direct link between dissociated neural reactivity profiles and computational parameters of impulsive decision-making (via DDM). The findings could inform the development of more targeted interventions, such as therapies that specifically modulate anticipatory versus consummatory processes.
proposals.novelty: While reward phase distinctions are known, this study uniquely integrates phase-specific fMRI with computational modeling of choice (DDM) to directly test how dissociated neural dynamics drive specific, maladaptive decision patterns like steep discounting, a link not yet firmly established pre-2026.

proposals.title: A Multimodal Predictive Model of Impulsivity: Integrating fMRI, Temporal Discounting, and Trait Conscientiousness for Enhanced Clinical Screening
proposals.motivation: Impulsivity is a transdiagnostic feature of many psychiatric disorders, but current clinical assessments are often subjective and lack predictive specificity. Pre-2026 research shows that delay discounting is a robust behavioral marker of impulsivity, and neural activity in valuation circuits (e.g., vmPFC, striatum) is associated with discounting rates. However, single-modality predictors have limited accuracy, and the optimal integration of neural, behavioral, and dispositional data for clinical screening remains unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: A multimodal model integrating fMRI-derived neural signals from reward and control networks, behavioral delay discounting parameters, and self-reported conscientiousness will significantly outperform any single predictor in classifying individuals with clinically relevant impulsive disorders and in predicting real-world impulsive choice reactivity.
proposals.method: We will recruit 150 participants, including individuals with disorders associated with impulsivity (e.g., ADHD, substance use) and healthy controls. Participants will complete a delay discounting task during fMRI scanning, a conscientiousness inventory, and a virtual reality-based reliability assessment of discounting. We will use a stacked generalization (ensemble) machine learning approach. Base learners (e.g., support vector machines, elastic net) will be trained on unimodal features: (1) fMRI activation patterns from medial temporal lobe and fronto-striatal circuits during choice, (2) drift-diffusion model parameters from discounting behavior, and (3) conscientiousness scores. A meta-learner (e.g., logistic regression) will then optimally combine these base predictions.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, multimodal predictive tool for impulsivity, moving beyond correlational studies to practical screening. It will establish the relative and combined predictive utility of neural, psychometric, and trait-level data, directly informing the development of more objective, efficient, and accurate clinical assessment protocols.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have linked these factors to impulsivity separately, this is the first to formally optimize their combination using ensemble machine learning for clinical prediction, explicitly testing whether a multimodal approach provides a necessary gain over established single-predictor models.

proposals.title: The Proximity Effect on Impulsive Choice is Domain-General: A Cross-Reward Comparison Using Drift Diffusion Modeling
proposals.motivation: Research by Peters and others (pre-2026) demonstrates that the physical proximity of food rewards increases impulsive choice and alters reaction time dynamics, potentially via increased approach motivation. However, it is unknown if this 'proximity effect' is specific to consummatory rewards or represents a general cognitive mechanism applicable to monetary, social, or drug-related cues. Understanding the boundary conditions of this effect is crucial for models of impulsivity, especially given its relevance to disorders like addiction and gambling, where cue reactivity is a core feature.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the proximity-induced increase in impulsive choice and the associated shift in decision dynamics (quantified by drift diffusion parameters) will generalize across qualitatively different reward types (monetary, social praise, and drug-related cues), indicating a domain-general effect of cue proximity on temporal discounting.
proposals.method: We will adapt a validated virtual reality temporal discounting task (leveraging Peters' prior VR reliability work) where participants make serial choices between smaller-immediate and larger-delayed rewards. Reward type (monetary points, social reward cues, neutral/drug-related images for at-risk populations) will be blocked. Crucially, in the 'Near' condition, the immediate reward cue will be rendered within peripersonal space using VR, while in the 'Far' condition, it will be beyond reach. We will employ hierarchical Bayesian drift diffusion modeling (HDDM) to decompose choices into latent components: drift rate (evidence accumulation speed), threshold (response caution), and non-decision time. This allows us to test if proximity uniformly affects the evidence accumulation process (drift rate) for different rewards.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct test of the domain-generality of the reward proximity effect, moving beyond food-specific paradigms. By integrating DDM, it will elucidate the computational mechanism (e.g., increased drift rate for immediate options) by which proximity biases choice across domains. The findings will critically inform theoretical models of impulsivity and have practical implications for understanding cue reactivity in clinical disorders.
proposals.novelty: While proximity effects are established for food, their existence and computational signature for abstract (social) and addiction-relevant (drug cues) rewards is untested. Combining a multi-reward VR proximity paradigm with formal cognitive modeling (DDM) to identify a common latent mechanism represents a significant methodological and conceptual advance over standard discounting analyses.

proposals.title: A Drift-Diffusion Model of Proximity-Dependent Discounting: Quantifying the Spatiotemporal Dynamics of Impulsive Choice
proposals.motivation: Research on temporal discounting shows that rewards that are near increase impulsive action, but the formal mechanisms linking physical proximity to valuation and response inhibition remain poorly specified. Integrating concepts from behavioral ecology, where spatial discounting models describe how distance reduces resource value, could provide a rigorous computational framework to unify findings across temporal and spatial domains of impulsive choice, particularly in disorders like addiction and ADHD.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the subjective value of a reward will be modulated by a hyperbolic discounting function of physical distance, analogous to temporal discounting, and that this proximity-dependent valuation will directly influence the drift rate parameter of a diffusion decision model, predicting both choice and reaction time patterns.
proposals.method: We will adapt a spatial foraging task in a validated virtual reality environment to measure choice and reaction times for rewards at varying distances. We will fit and compare competing computational models: 1) a standard hyperbolic temporal discounting model, 2) a hyperbolic spatial discounting model, and 3) an integrated spatiotemporal model. The winning valuation model will then be used to parameterize a hierarchical drift-diffusion model (HDDM), testing if trial-wise drift rates are predicted by the spatially discounted value difference between options.
proposals.contribution: This work will formally establish a quantitative model of proximity-dependent discounting, bridging behavioral ecology and neuroeconomics. It will provide a novel, process-level account of how spatial cues bias evidence accumulation toward impulsive action, enhancing the reliability and interpretability of discounting measures in clinical and VR-based studies.
proposals.novelty: While spatial effects on impulsivity are documented, this study is the first to formally apply and test spatial discounting functions from ecology in a human choice paradigm and to link them mechanistically to evidence accumulation dynamics via drift-diffusion modeling.

proposals.title: Within-Session Dynamics of Learning Rate in Gambling Tasks: A Hierarchical Drift-Diffusion Model Analysis of Attention and Motivation
proposals.motivation: Computational models of choice, such as reinforcement learning and drift-diffusion models (DDM), often assume stable learning or evidence accumulation rates across a session. This temporal homogeneity assumption may be violated in clinical populations like gamblers, where attention and motivation are known to fluctuate, potentially biasing estimates of core constructs like temporal discounting and risk sensitivity. Understanding these dynamics is crucial for accurately characterizing the cognitive mechanisms of disordered gambling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that gamblers, compared to controls, will exhibit greater within-session variability in the learning rate (or drift rate) parameter during a sequential gambling task, and that this variability will be systematically associated with trial-by-trial measures of attentional engagement and self-reported motivation.
proposals.method: We will employ a hierarchical Bayesian version of the DDM, where the drift rate (v) is allowed to vary as a Gaussian random walk across trials within a session, capturing non-stationary evidence accumulation. Participants (gamblers and controls) will complete a virtual reality gambling task with integrated eye-tracking (pupillometry as a proxy for arousal/attention) and intermittent self-report prompts for motivation. Model parameters will be estimated using Hamiltonian Monte Carlo in Stan, and the estimated trial-wise drift rate trajectories will be regressed against the physiological and self-report measures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodological framework for detecting and quantifying within-session cognitive dynamics in choice tasks. It will yield new insights into the real-time interplay between attention, motivation, and decision processes in gambling disorder, moving beyond static trait-level explanations. The findings could inform the development of more sensitive computational biomarkers for clinical reactivity and state-dependent risk.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have associated disorder with parameter differences, explicitly modeling the trial-by-trial *dynamics* of a core parameter like learning/drift rate within a hierarchical Bayesian DDM framework is novel. This relaxes the critical but rarely tested assumption of temporal homogeneity in computational psychiatry.

proposals.title: Dissecting Polygenic Mechanisms of Reinforcement Learning: A Computational Phenotyping and cis-eQTL Analysis of the DRD2 Locus
proposals.motivation: Genetic associations with reward-related behaviors and disorders are often mapped to broad loci like DRD2, but the specific computational mechanisms and causal genes remain unclear. This limits the development of targeted interventions. Pre-2026 work shows that reinforcement learning (RL) models can decompose behavior and neural signals into components like prediction error, but these computational phenotypes have not been rigorously linked to fine-mapped genetic variation and gene expression within a locus.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that genetic variation in the DRD2 locus will show dissociable associations with specific RL computational components (e.g., prediction error sensitivity, learning rate), and that these associations will not be exclusive to DRD2 expression but will be polygenic, involving other genes in the locus through shared regulatory mechanisms.
proposals.method: We will apply hierarchical Bayesian RL models (e.g., a Rescorla-Wagner or hybrid model with separate learning rates for positive/negative prediction errors) to existing behavioral and fMRI data from reward learning tasks in genotyped participants. Model-derived trial-by-trial estimates of prediction error and expected value will be extracted as phenotypes. These computational phenotypes will then be tested for association with genetic variants in the DRD2 locus and, crucially, with cis-eQTL data for gene expression in dopaminergic pathways, using fine-mapping and colocalization analyses to assess specificity.
proposals.contribution: This work will move beyond simple gene-behavior associations to identify which specific computational processes are influenced by genetic variation. It will critically test the dominant 'DRD2-centric' model of reward dysfunction and provide a framework for polygenic dissection of other complex behavioral loci.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct integration of trial-by-trial computational phenotypes from RL models with cis-eQTL fine-mapping to test for polygenic effects within a single locus, moving from associating genes with behavior to associating genes with specific algorithmic components of learning.

proposals.title: Dissociating the Neural Mechanisms of Reward Anticipation and Consumption in Temporal Discounting and Gambling Choice
proposals.motivation: Neuroeconomic models of intertemporal and risky choice often conflate the neural signals of anticipating a reward with those of its consumption or outcome evaluation. This conflation limits our understanding of the core mechanisms driving impulsive and disordered decision-making, such as in gambling disorder. Pre-2026, studies using fMRI have linked ventral striatal BOLD activity during anticipation to discounting behavior, but it remains unclear if this signal is a unique driver or is confounded by post-outcome learning signals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that BOLD signal in the ventral striatum and medial temporal lobe during the reward anticipation phase, but not during the consumption or outcome phase, will be uniquely associated with individual differences in temporal discounting rates and gambling-related choices, even when controlling for learning-related prediction errors.
proposals.method: We will recruit 80 participants to complete a novel fMRI task combining temporal discounting and simple gambling trials within a virtual reality environment to enhance ecological validity. The task will temporally separate anticipation, consumption, and outcome evaluation phases. We will use a hierarchical Bayesian variant of the drift-diffusion model (DDM) to jointly estimate decision parameters and neural reactivity. A key analysis will employ multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) in medial temporal lobe subregions and the ventral striatum to test for the incidental reinstatement of reward-value codes specifically during the anticipation phase, dissociating it from subsequent phases.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct test of the specific role of anticipation-phase activity in value-based choice, moving beyond correlational evidence. It will clarify the neural pathophysiology of impulsivity by identifying which processing stage is most aberrant in traits associated with disordered decision-making. The integration of computational modeling (DDM) with high-resolution fMRI analysis offers a novel framework for linking neural mechanisms to choice dynamics.
proposals.novelty: The proposal's novelty lies in its rigorous temporal dissociation of reward processing stages within the same task and its use of MVPA-based reinstatement analyses—a technique common in memory research—to track value representations with high temporal specificity in decision-making contexts, which is non-obvious in standard discounting studies.

proposals.title: A Drift-Diffusion Model of Disclosure: Dopaminergic Precision Predicts Trial-Level Reliability of Adolescent Smoking Self-Report
proposals.motivation: Adolescent smoking self-report is plagued by social desirability bias, undermining the validity of epidemiological and clinical studies. Current methods treat reports as binary true/false, ignoring the underlying cognitive dynamics of the disclosure decision. This project aims to reframe self-report as a noisy decision process, leveraging neurocomputational models to quantify the moment-to-moment reliability of each answer.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the reliability of an adolescent's smoking disclosure is governed by the precision of a value comparison process, implemented via dopaminergic modulation of decision noise, such that lower decision precision leads to more stochastic and less reliable self-reports.
proposals.method: Adolescent participants (smokers and non-smokers) will complete a smoking disclosure task in a virtual reality environment to enhance ecological validity and measure behavioral reactivity. We will fit a hierarchical drift-diffusion model (DDM) to their yes/no responses, where drift rate variability (η) quantifies trial-level decision precision. Pupillometry will serve as a proxy for locus coeruleus-norepinephrine (LC-NE) activity, a system linked to decision noise and modulated by dopamine. We will test for associations between model-estimated precision, pupil dilation, and known markers of social desirability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational framework for assessing self-report data, generating a trial-by-trial probability of answer reliability rather than a binary truth value. It will identify a putative neurocomputational biomarker (decision precision) for disclosure bias, with applications for improving measurement in substance use and other sensitive behavioral domains.
proposals.novelty: This approach moves beyond treating bias as a static trait, instead modeling it as a dynamic, neurally implemented fluctuation in decision noise. It fuses Peters' expertise in temporal decision-making (drift models, VR assessment) with a novel application to the computational psychiatry of disclosure.

proposals.title: Genetic Modulation of Social Decision Dynamics: A Drift Diffusion Analysis of TTC12-ANKK1-DRD2 Effects on Peer-Influenced Explore-Exploit Choices
proposals.motivation: Smoking initiation is a critical public health problem strongly influenced by peer behavior and genetic susceptibility. Pre-2026 research has established links between the TTC12-ANKK1-DRD2 gene cluster, nicotine dependence, and reward processing, and separately, the role of social learning in substance use. However, the precise cognitive-computational mechanisms through which genetic risk and social cues interact to drive real-time decisions to try smoking remain unknown.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that individuals carrying risk variants in the TTC12-ANKK1-DRD2 cluster will exhibit altered drift diffusion model parameters—specifically, a higher drift rate (increased sensitivity) to peer smoking evidence and/or a lower decision threshold—during explore-exploit decisions in socially salient contexts, revealing a mechanistic pathway for gene-environment interaction.
proposals.method: We will recruit 400 young adult participants, genotyped for TTC12-ANKK1-DRD2 variants, to complete a novel explore-exploit task in a virtual reality environment displaying simulated peer cues. Participants repeatedly choose between exploring a novel activity with uncertain payoff (simulating smoking initiation) or exploiting a known small reward. Behavioral choice and reaction time data will be fitted using hierarchical Bayesian drift diffusion models (DDM) via the HDDM or similar toolbox. Core DDM parameters—drift rate (v), boundary separation (a), and non-decision time (t0)—will be analyzed as a function of genetic status and peer cue condition.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a formal, process-level account of how a well-established genetic risk factor biases the temporal dynamics of social decision-making toward harmful exploration. It moves beyond correlational associations to identify specific computational parameters (e.g., evidence accumulation rate) that are altered by gene-environment interplay, offering novel targets for mechanistic theory and precision prevention.
proposals.novelty: Fusing genetic epidemiology with trial-by-trial computational modeling of social explore-exploit decisions represents a significant advance over standard behavioral genetic studies, which typically examine aggregate outcomes rather than the latent decision process itself.

proposals.title: Decomposing Reward Anticipation in Temporal Discounting and Gambling Tasks: A Drift Diffusion and Reinforcement Learning Model Fusion
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research in decision neuroscience often treats reward anticipation as a monolithic signal in fMRI studies, yet it likely comprises distinct computational components like expected value and prediction error. This conflation limits our understanding of disorders characterized by impulsive choice, such as gambling disorder, where maladaptive decisions may stem from aberrant valuation or learning processes. A precise decomposition is needed to link neural reactivity to specific computational dysfunctions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that neural signals in the ventral striatum and medial prefrontal cortex during reward anticipation in temporal discounting and gambling tasks can be effectively decomposed into distinct prediction error and expected value components using model-based fMRI. Furthermore, we predict that individual differences in the weighting of these components will correlate with behavioral measures of impulsivity and task-related reactivity.
proposals.method: We will recruit participants to complete a novel virtual reality task combining temporal discounting and risky gambling elements, building on Peters' recent methodological work. Behavioral choices will be fit with hybrid computational models, including a Temporal Difference Reinforcement Learning (TDRL) model to estimate trial-by-trial expected values and prediction errors, and a Hierarchical Drift Diffusion Model (HDDM) to capture choice dynamics and reaction time. These trial-wise regressors will then be used in a model-based fMRI analysis to identify distinct neural substrates for each computational signal.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational account of the neural signals underlying reward anticipation, moving beyond simple cue reactivity. It will establish a direct link between specific reinforcement learning parameters, neural activity, and behavioral impulsivity metrics. The findings could inform targeted interventions for disorders of impulsivity by identifying whether dysfunctional anticipation is primarily driven by faulty prediction or valuation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of TDRL and HDDM frameworks within a single task and analysis pipeline to dissect anticipation, and the application of this approach to directly compare processes in discounting and gambling within the same participants, which is non-trivial given their different theoretical emphases on delay and risk.

proposals.title: Circuit-level mapping of nucleus accumbens deep brain stimulation effects on delay discounting using simultaneous fMRI
proposals.motivation: Deep brain stimulation (DBS) of the nucleus accumbens (NAc) is an emerging therapy for severe, treatment-refractory disorders involving impulsivity, such as obsessive-compulsive disorder. However, its precise circuit-level mechanisms for modulating temporal choice remain poorly understood. Combining DBS with functional neuroimaging during a delay discounting (DD) task offers a unique opportunity to directly observe how focal neuromodulation alters network-wide activity associated with impulsive decision-making.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that NAc-DBS, when active, will reduce behavioral discounting rates and that this effect will be mediated by DBS-induced modulation of functional connectivity between the NAc and key nodes of the valuation and cognitive control networks, specifically the ventromedial prefrontal cortex and dorsolateral prefrontal cortex.
proposals.method: We will recruit participants (N=15-20) with implanted NAc-DBS systems for OCD. In a within-subjects design, participants will perform a computerized delay discounting task during simultaneous 3T fMRI scanning under DBS-ON and DBS-OFF conditions, counterbalanced. Task-evoked BOLD responses and psychophysiological interaction (PPI) analyses will be used to identify DBS-modulated activity and connectivity. Computational modeling (e.g., hyperbolic discounting, drift-diffusion models) will quantify choice behavior and latent decision variables (e.g., drift rate), which will be correlated with neural measures.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct evidence of how focal NAc stimulation alters whole-brain network dynamics during intertemporal choice in humans. It will link a therapeutic neuromodulation technique to specific circuit mechanisms underlying discounting, informing both the pathophysiology of impulsivity-related disorders and the refinement of DBS targeting and parameter settings.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have examined DBS or fMRI in isolation for discounting, the simultaneous combination in a patient cohort is novel and allows for causal inference about the NAc's role. Furthermore, integrating computational psychiatry models (drift-diffusion) with circuit-level neuroimaging during active stimulation moves beyond simple localization to test mechanistic models of choice.

proposals.title: Modeling Cue-Induced Impulsivity: A Drift-Diffusion Framework for Pavlovian-Instrumental Transfer in Reward-Related Disorders
proposals.motivation: Cue-induced craving is a core feature of substance use and gambling disorders, driving relapse and maladaptive choice. Pre-2026 computational psychiatry has separately studied temporal discounting (impulsivity) and Pavlovian-instrumental transfer (PIT), but lacks a unified model to explain how reward-associated cues hijack value-based decision-making processes, leading to increased discounting and impulsive action. This gap limits our formal understanding of a key clinical phenomenon.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that exposure to reward-predictive cues will induce a Pavlovian bias in a hierarchical reinforcement learning model, which will be formally characterized by a shift in the drift rate of a subsequent drift-diffusion model (DDM) during temporal discounting and gambling tasks, explaining increased behavioral impulsivity and choice stochasticity.
proposals.method: We will recruit participants with varying levels of problematic gambling or substance use. In a multi-session virtual reality study, participants will first undergo a Pavlovian conditioning phase. They will then perform a validated temporal discounting and a risky choice task in VR, with task-irrelevant conditioned cues presented incidentally. We will fit choices using a hybrid model: a PIT-modulated Q-learning algorithm to generate trial-wise value differences, which will serve as the drift rate input to a hierarchical Bayesian DDM. Model comparison will assess if cue exposure alters drift rates, thresholds, or non-decision time.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, mechanistic link between cue-reactivity and choice dysregulation, moving beyond correlation to computational causality. It will deliver a novel, validated computational assay (the cue-modulated DDM) for quantifying individual differences in cue vulnerability, with direct relevance for developing targeted interventions. The study will also advance the reliability assessment of computational measures in ecologically rich VR environments.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific integration of a PIT process as a direct modulator of the evidence accumulation process in the DDM, applied to disorder-relevant populations in a controlled VR setting, which bridges learning theory and sequential sampling models of choice.

proposals.title: Modulating vmPFC-Striatal Discounting Pathways with Episodic Future Thinking in Substance Use Disorders: A Comparative fMRI Study
proposals.motivation: Episodic Future Thinking (EFT) has shown promise in reducing delay discounting in obesity and gambling disorder by enhancing vmPFC valuation of future rewards. However, its neural efficacy in substance use disorders (SUDs), where vmPFC-striatal dysfunction is pronounced, remains untested. A direct comparison across disorders is needed to determine if EFT's therapeutic potential is general or varies with the primary reinforcer (e.g., drugs vs. money).
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that EFT will reduce delay discounting in individuals with SUDs, but will engage a distinct pattern of vmPFC-striatal modulation compared to gambling disorder, reflecting disorder-specific alterations in reward valuation circuitry.
proposals.method: We will recruit participants with stimulant use disorder, gambling disorder, and matched controls. Using a within-subjects fMRI design, participants will complete a temporal discounting task under two conditions: a neutral cue condition and an EFT condition where future rewards are paired with personalized, vivid episodic tags. Neural data will be analyzed using a combination of general linear models (GLMs) to identify condition-specific activation and dynamic causal modeling (DCM) to compare effective connectivity between the vmPFC and ventral striatum across groups and conditions.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct evidence of how EFT modulates core discounting neural circuits in SUDs. It will clarify whether EFT's mechanism is transdiagnostic or disorder-specific, informing targeted cognitive interventions for addiction. The DCM analysis will offer novel insights into the directed functional interactions disrupted by different addictive reinforcers.
proposals.novelty: While EFT has been applied to discounting, its comparative neurobiological effects across addiction types are unknown. The use of effective connectivity modeling (DCM) to test for disorder-specific changes in vmPFC-striatal pathways during EFT represents a significant methodological advance beyond standard activation analyses.

proposals.title: Context-Dependent Generalization of Functional Connectivity: A Comparison of Resting-State and Value-Based Decision Networks in Healthy and Gambling-Disorder Participants
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 neuroimaging research often assumes that functional connectivity measured during a specific cognitive task (e.g., temporal discounting) reflects stable, intrinsic resting-state circuits. This assumption underpins clinical and computational models of disorders like gambling disorder, which is associated with altered reward and impulsivity networks. However, if task-based connectivity is context-specific and does not generalize, this could limit the translational validity of task-fMRI findings for understanding baseline neural dysfunction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that functional connectivity within core valuation and cognitive control networks (e.g., ventral striatum-ventromedial prefrontal cortex, fronto-parietal) measured during a temporal discounting and gambling task will show significant, state-dependent deviations from resting-state connectivity patterns, and that this task-rest divergence will be more pronounced in individuals with gambling disorder.
proposals.method: We will recruit 60 participants (30 with gambling disorder, 30 matched controls). Each will undergo resting-state fMRI and a task-fMRI session involving a well-validated temporal discounting and risky choice paradigm. Functional connectivity will be computed using psychophysiological interaction (PPI) analysis for task data and seed-based correlation for resting-state data. Key contrasts will compare connectivity matrices (e.g., using the Network-Based Statistic toolbox) between states (task vs. rest) and groups. We will also employ a drift-diffusion model framework on behavioral choice data to extract computational parameters (e.g., drift rate, decision threshold) and test their association with neural reactivity in connectivity patterns.
proposals.contribution: This study will directly test a foundational assumption in clinical and decision neuroscience regarding the generalizability of task-evoked connectivity. It will clarify whether alterations observed in gambling disorder during decision-making are state-bound or reflect trait-like circuit dysfunction. The findings will inform the design and interpretation of future biomarker studies and targeted interventions.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have examined task-rest correspondence in healthy individuals, this proposal uniquely integrates Dr. Peters' expertise in discounting and gambling to test this correspondence in a clinical disorder characterized by impulsive choice, using a combined computational psychiatry and connectivity reactivity framework.

proposals.title: Directed Connectivity Between Striatum and Amygdala During Temporal Discounting: A Dynamic Causal Modeling Study
proposals.motivation: The striatum and amygdala are core nodes in valuation and affective processing during intertemporal choice, but the directionality of their interaction remains ambiguous. Pre-2026 lesion and fMRI studies show both regions are associated with discounting behavior and reactivity to reward immediacy, yet effective connectivity analyses are rare. Determining if the amygdala drives striatal value signals or vice versa is crucial for refining neurocomputational models of impulsivity and disorders like addiction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that during choices involving immediate rewards, effective connectivity will show a dominant directional influence from the amygdala to the striatum, reflecting the amygdala's role in amplifying the salience of proximate rewards. This amygdala-to-striatum drive will be stronger in individuals with steeper discounting rates.
proposals.method: We will collect fMRI data from 50 participants performing a well-validated temporal discounting task with gambling elements. Subject-specific anatomical ROIs for ventral striatum and basolateral amygdala will be defined. We will apply Dynamic Causal Modeling (DCM) for fMRI, specifically using a bilinear deterministic model within the SPM12 framework. We will compare families of models representing all possible directed connections between these regions, modulated by the immediate reward condition, and use Bayesian model selection to identify the most likely effective connectivity architecture.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first causal account of striatum-amygdala circuitry during discounting, moving beyond correlation. It will identify a potential neural mechanism for heightened reactivity to immediate rewards. The findings will inform theoretical models by specifying the hierarchical flow of information that underlies impulsive choice.
proposals.novelty: While DCM has been applied in memory and perception, its application to dissect directionality in core reward circuits during discounting is novel. Combining Dr. Peters' expertise in discounting measures and virtual reality reliability with a cutting-edge, model-based connectivity analysis represents a significant methodological advance for the field.

proposals.title: Striatal-Amygdala Functional Connectivity Mediates Reinforcement Learning Parameters in Temporal Discounting and Gambling Tasks
proposals.motivation: Computational models of reinforcement learning (RL) have successfully parsed choice behavior into parameters like learning rate and temporal discounting factor. However, the neural circuits that give rise to these parameters, particularly the interaction between reward valuation (striatum) and affective salience (amygdala), remain poorly understood. Linking model-based parameters to dynamic brain connectivity is crucial for understanding disorders characterized by impulsive choice, such as addiction and gambling disorder.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that individual differences in RL parameters, specifically a steeper temporal discounting rate and a higher learning rate for rewards, will be associated with stronger task-modulated functional connectivity between the ventral striatum and the amygdala. This coupling will be most pronounced during uncertain, high-magnitude reward outcomes.
proposals.method: We will recruit 80 participants to complete a novel decision-making task in an fMRI scanner, integrating temporal discounting and probabilistic gambling elements. Behavior will be fit using a hybrid RL model (Q-learning with a hyperbolic discounting function) to extract participant-specific parameters. We will then perform psychophysiological interaction (PPI) analysis to quantify task-dependent functional connectivity between a priori striatal and amygdala seeds. Finally, we will use multi-level mediation models to test if striatal-amygdala connectivity mediates the relationship between model parameters and real-world measures of impulsivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, circuit-level explanation for key computational parameters in decision-making. It will bridge the gap between abstract RL models and systems neuroscience, offering new biomarkers for the dysregulated reward processing seen in impulsivity-related disorders. The findings will also inform the reliability of model-based fMRI approaches in clinical contexts.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have linked RL parameters to regional BOLD activity, this proposal uniquely tests the hypothesis that the *interaction* between specific brain regions is the critical neural substrate for these parameters. It integrates Dr. Peters' expertise in temporal discounting measures and virtual reality reliability assessment with cutting-edge, pre-2026 model-based connectivity analysis.

proposals.title: A Pharmacological fMRI Study of Tolcapone's Effects on Neural Value Computation During Intertemporal Choice
proposals.motivation: Tolcapone, a catechol-O-methyltransferase (COMT) inhibitor, modulates prefrontal dopamine and has been shown to reduce temporal discounting in behavioral studies. However, its precise neural mechanism of action on the core computational processes of value representation and comparison remains poorly understood. Mapping these effects is crucial for developing targeted pharmacological interventions for disorders characterized by impulsivity and poor future planning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that tolcapone will increase the neural representation of future reward value in the ventromedial prefrontal cortex (vmPFC) and enhance the trial-by-trial correlation between model-derived decision variables (e.g., discounted value difference) and activity in the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (dlPFC) during choice.
proposals.method: We will conduct a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled fMRI study where participants complete a temporal discounting task. We will employ model-based fMRI, using hierarchical Bayesian parameter estimation to fit a hyperbolic discounting model to each participant's choices. The trial-wise regressors for the fMRI GLM analysis will include the computationally derived subjective value of each option and the decision variable (value difference). We will compare the strength and spatial extent of these neural representations between the tolcapone and placebo groups, controlling for potential drift-diffusion model parameters of choice reactivity.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a direct, computational-level map of how a dopamine-enhancing drug alters the neural circuitry of intertemporal choice. It will bridge the gap between behavioral pharmacology and computational psychiatry, offering a mechanistic account of tolcapone's effects. The findings will inform theories of prefrontal dopamine's role in value-based decision-making and could guide future clinical applications.
proposals.novelty: While tolcapone's behavioral effects are known, applying model-based fMRI regressors to isolate its impact on specific computational stages of valuation is novel. This approach moves beyond associating drug effects with regional activation to testing its influence on the algorithmic implementation of choice.

proposals.title: Paradigm-Dependent Noise: A Drift Diffusion Model Analysis of How Episodic Future Thinking Modulates Choice Consistency in Temporal Discounting
proposals.motivation: Episodic Future Thinking (EFT) is a promising intervention for reducing delay discounting, but its effects on choice consistency (decision noise) are small and inconsistent across studies. This inconsistency limits theoretical understanding and practical application. Resolving it is critical for determining when and how EFT robustly shapes decision-making processes.
proposals.hypothesis: The effect of EFT on choice consistency is moderated by task parameters that influence cognitive load and reward reactivity, such as reward magnitude and the temporal proximity of delays. Specifically, EFT will reduce decision noise most effectively in paradigms with high-magnitude rewards and proximal delays, where it can counteract associated impulsive reactivity.
proposals.method: We will conduct a within-subjects study (N=120) using a virtual reality temporal discounting task with validated reliability. Participants will complete EFT and control conditions across multiple blocks varying reward magnitude and delay framing. We will fit hierarchical Bayesian Drift Diffusion Models (DDM) to trial-by-trial choices, estimating drift rate (v) and boundary separation (a) as indices of evidence accumulation speed and response caution/consistency. Model comparison will test if EFT's effect on boundary separation is modulated by our experimental factors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a computational mechanism (via DDM parameters) for EFT's variable effects, moving beyond descriptive inconsistency. It will identify boundary conditions for EFT's efficacy, offering clearer guidelines for its use in clinical and experimental settings focused on disorders of impulsivity.
proposals.novelty: While DDMs are used in discounting, applying them to dissect the *inconsistent* noise effects of EFT across manipulated task paradigms is novel. This integrates Peters' expertise in discounting, reactivity, and virtual reality assessment to solve a specific puzzle in the literature.

proposals.title: Episodic Simulation as a State-Construction Process: A Model-Based Reinforcement Learning Account of Dynamic Temporal Discounting
proposals.motivation: Standard temporal discounting models treat subjective value as a static function of delay, failing to capture moment-to-moment fluctuations in valuation observed in real-world choice and gambling contexts. Pre-2026 research by Peters and others links these fluctuations to episodic simulation and reactivity, processes associated with medial temporal lobe function, but lacks a formal computational framework to describe how simulation dynamically updates value.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that episodic simulation during a discounting task serves as a model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) process, where mentally sampling future states (e.g., reward consumption) constructs a richer state representation, dynamically reducing the effective temporal distance and thereby increasing the subjective value of delayed rewards.
proposals.method: We will develop and test a novel MBRL agent where a recurrent network (e.g., an LSTM) learns to generate simulated episodic sequences of future states from a 'current delay' state. This simulation output will be integrated into a successor representation to dynamically update state values. The model will be fit to trial-by-trial choice data from a novel virtual reality discounting task with intermittent thought probes, assessing its ability to predict choice reactivity and the drift in preference associated with simulation episodes, compared to static hyperbolic and quasi-hyperbolic discounting models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal RL account of how episodic cognition directly shapes intertemporal choice, moving beyond correlation to a mechanistic explanation. It will yield a new, computationally precise metric for 'simulation-induced discount rate modulation,' with implications for understanding disorders of impulsivity and for improving the reliability of discounting measures in ecological settings.
proposals.novelty: While MBRL has been applied to spatial navigation and planning, its transfer to model the *internal cognitive process* of episodic future thought during valuation is novel. This approach uniquely formalizes simulation as a state-construction mechanism within the reward discounting framework, a synthesis not present in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: The Effect of Future Event Valence on Temporal Discounting and Subjective Connectedness
proposals.motivation: Dominant theories of temporal discounting, such as the future self-continuity hypothesis, posit that positive future anticipation reduces discounting by enhancing connectedness to one's future self. However, this framework conflates the effects of temporal connectedness with positive valence. It remains unknown whether negative or neutral future events can similarly reduce discounting by increasing temporal connectedness, independent of positive affect. This distinction is crucial for developing a mechanistic understanding of intertemporal choice and for designing interventions for disorders like addiction and impulsivity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that vividly imagining negative or neutral future events will reduce temporal discounting rates to a similar degree as positive events, and that this effect will be mediated by increased subjective temporal connectedness, not by positive valence.
proposals.method: We will recruit 200 participants for a within-subjects virtual reality (VR) study. Using a validated VR environment for temporal discounting assessment, participants will complete discounting tasks after VR-based episodic future thinking inductions for positive, negative, and neutral events. We will employ hierarchical Bayesian parameter estimation to model discounting rates (k) and drift-diffusion modeling (DDM) to analyze choice dynamics. Subjective measures of valence, arousal, and temporal connectedness (e.g., the Consideration of Future Consequences scale) will be collected to test for mediation.
proposals.contribution: This work will challenge the assumption that positive valence is necessary for future-oriented interventions to reduce discounting. It will provide a refined theoretical model separating the roles of valence and temporal connectedness. Methodologically, it will extend the use of VR and computational modeling (DDM) to study the mechanisms of episodic future thinking on choice.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links positive future thinking to reduced discounting, the specific causal role of valence versus connectedness is untested. Proposing that negative events can have a similar effect by boosting connectedness is a counterintuitive departure from the prevailing reward-based framework.

proposals.title: Dissociating Social Desirability from Temporal Preference: A Drift-Diffusion and Demand Reactivity Study of Adolescent Discounting
proposals.motivation: A robust literature documents developmental shifts in temporal discounting, with adolescents often showing greater patience than children. However, these findings may be confounded by social demand characteristics, as adolescents are highly sensitive to peer and experimenter perceptions. If choices reflect a desire to appear mature rather than genuine preference, our understanding of adolescent decision-making and associated neurodevelopmental disorders is fundamentally flawed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant portion of the observed increase in choice of larger-later rewards in adolescents, compared to children, is driven by social desirability and demand characteristics, not by an intrinsic shift in time preference. This confound will be evident in choice process data (reaction time, decision uncertainty) and will diminish under conditions of reduced evaluative concern.
proposals.method: We will recruit child (8-12) and adolescent (13-17) participants for a temporal discounting task administered in a validated virtual reality (VR) environment to enhance ecological validity and measure reliability. Using a between-subjects design, we will manipulate demand characteristics: a High-Demand condition will emphasize 'mature' choices, while a Low-Demand condition will use covert, gamified framing. We will apply the Hierarchical Drift-Diffusion Model (HDDM) to trial-level choice and reaction time data to separate the drift rate (evidence accumulation for the larger-later option) from a priori response bias, isolating the social desirability component.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first process-level evidence quantifying the influence of demand characteristics on developmental discounting measures. It will refine models of adolescent decision-making by identifying a key social confound and validate improved methodological safeguards (VR gamification, computational modeling) for future clinical and developmental studies of impulsivity and disorders associated with discounting anomalies.
proposals.novelty: While demand characteristics are a known issue, their specific, quantified impact on developmental discounting trajectories is unexamined. The novel integration of an explicit demand manipulation with computational modeling of choice dynamics (drift-diffusion) in a controlled VR setting moves beyond simple self-report to mechanistically dissociate social reactivity from genuine temporal preference.

proposals.title: Dynamic Attention in Deliberation: Modeling Non-Stationary Drift Rates to Capture Attribute-Specific Attentional Shifts in Temporal Discounting
proposals.motivation: Drift-diffusion models (DDMs) are central to understanding value-based choice, but they typically assume a constant drift rate within a trial, implying stable evidence accumulation. This fails to capture the dynamic nature of deliberation, where attention may shift between different attributes (e.g., reward magnitude vs. delay) over time. Understanding these intra-trial dynamics is crucial for linking computational models to neural processes of attention and valuation, particularly in disorders characterized by choice impulsivity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the drift rate in a temporal discounting task is non-stationary, fluctuating in a way that reflects moment-to-moment shifts in attention between immediate and delayed reward attributes, and that the volatility of these shifts will be associated with individual differences in impulsivity and task reactivity.
proposals.method: We will fit a novel DDM variant where the drift rate μ(t) is modeled as a latent stochastic process (e.g., an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process) within each trial, estimated via particle filtering methods. Participants will complete a virtual reality temporal discounting task with integrated eye-tracking to provide a ground-truth measure of attentional shifts (fixations on reward vs. delay information). Model parameters capturing drift rate dynamics will be correlated with behavioral measures of discounting, self-reported impulsivity, and pupillometric indices of cognitive effort.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new computational framework for analyzing the fine-grained dynamics of decision-making, moving beyond average trial summaries. It will directly test the link between attentional shifts, evidence accumulation, and impulsive choice, offering a more mechanistic account of deliberation that can inform studies of disorders affecting decision-making.
proposals.novelty: While time-varying drift has been explored in perception, applying it to model attentional shifts between distinct value attributes in a complex economic choice task is novel. Integrating this model with eye-tracking in a validated virtual reality discounting paradigm creates a powerful multi-method testbed.

proposals.title: Neural Correlates of Evidence Accumulation Dynamics: Trial-by-Trial fMRI Predictors of Drift Rate in Temporal Discounting
proposals.motivation: Computational models like the drift-diffusion model (DDM) decompose value-based choices into latent parameters such as drift rate, which quantifies the efficiency of evidence accumulation. While drift rate is a critical determinant of decision speed and accuracy, its neural basis remains poorly understood, especially at the trial-by-trial level. Linking moment-to-moment neural activity to trial-wise variations in drift rate would bridge a critical gap between computational theory and neurobiology, offering a mechanistic account of how the brain implements evidence integration during intertemporal and risky choice.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that trial-wise fluctuations in the BOLD signal within a core valuation and evidence accumulation network—including ventromedial prefrontal cortex (vmPFC), anterior cingulate cortex (ACC), and intraparietal sulcus (IPS)—will significantly predict trial-by-trial estimates of drift rate derived from a hierarchical DDM applied to a temporal discounting task.
proposals.method: We will collect fMRI data from 50 participants performing a well-validated temporal discounting task with intermixed gamble trials. A hierarchical Bayesian DDM will be fitted to choice reaction time data to extract trial-level posterior estimates of drift rate for each participant. These trial-wise drift rates will serve as parametric modulators in a general linear model (GLM) analysis of the fMRI data. We will use a whole-brain searchlight multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) with support vector regression (SVR) to identify brain regions where multivoxel activity patterns predict drift rate variations. Connectivity analyses (psychophysiological interaction, PPI) will test if trial-wise drift rate modulates functional coupling between vmPFC and dorsolateral prefrontal cortex.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct neural signature of trial-by-trial variations in evidence accumulation efficiency during value-based choice, moving beyond static neural correlates of value. It will validate and refine the neurobiological interpretation of a core DDM parameter. Furthermore, by examining both discounting and gambling, it will reveal whether a common or distinct neural circuitry governs evidence accumulation for different types of future and risky outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have associated neural activity with choice or value, predicting a trial-wise, model-derived *process* parameter (drift rate) from fMRI is a novel and more direct test of evidence accumulation theories. Combining hierarchical DDM trial estimates with MVPA for prediction moves beyond standard GLM approaches, capturing distributed neural patterns that govern decision dynamics.

proposals.title: Single-Neuron Drift-Diffusion Dynamics in Primate Orbitofrontal Cortex During Intertemporal Choice
proposals.motivation: Drift-diffusion models (DDMs) are the dominant computational framework for explaining the dynamics of value-based choice, including temporal discounting, but they are inferred from behavior. Direct neural evidence for the proposed evidence accumulation process at the cellular level is lacking, creating a critical gap between algorithmic models and their biological implementation. This study aims to bridge this gap by testing if single-neuron activity in a key valuation region, the orbitofrontal cortex (OFC), exhibits the hallmark firing rate dynamics of a DDM during discounting decisions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that populations of single neurons in primate OFC will exhibit firing rate trajectories consistent with a drift-diffusion process, where the drift rate is proportional to the subjective value difference between immediate and delayed rewards, and the decision boundary correlates with choice commitment.
proposals.method: We will record single-unit activity from OFC in non-human primates performing a well-established temporal discounting task with variable reward magnitudes and delays. Neural trajectories will be analyzed using a combination of linear discriminant analysis to identify choice-predictive dimensions and generalized linear models to relate firing rates to trial-by-trial subjective value differences derived from behavioral discount functions. We will fit hierarchical DDMs to behavior and compare the trial-by-trial drift rates and decision boundaries to the fitted neural dynamics using cross-validated regression and mutual information analyses.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, cellular-resolution test of DDM dynamics during economic choice within OFC, a region heavily associated with subjective value representation. It will establish a causal link between single-neuron firing patterns and the computational primitives of evidence accumulation, moving beyond correlational value coding. The findings will constrain future biophysical models of decision-making and inform theories of impulsivity and disorders like addiction, where discounting is altered.
proposals.novelty: While DDMs are applied to discounting behavior and OFC value signals are well-documented, directly observing DDM-like accumulation dynamics in single OFC neurons during an intertemporal choice task is a novel and critical test. This moves beyond demonstrating that neural activity correlates with value to showing it implements the specific dynamic comparison process postulated by leading psychological models.

proposals.title: Caudate Activation Drives Evidence Accumulation: Trial-Level fMRI Links to Drift Rate in Temporal Discounting
proposals.motivation: The drift diffusion model (DDM) applied to intertemporal choice dissociates decision components like evidence accumulation speed (drift rate) from non-decision time, offering a mechanistic window into impulsivity. However, the neural substrates of these trial-by-trial computational parameters remain poorly specified, limiting our understanding of how brain dynamics give rise to specific choice pathologies. Linking trial-level neural activity to DDM parameters can bridge the gap between computational psychiatry and cognitive neuroscience.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that trial-level BOLD signal in the caudate nucleus, a region central to reward valuation and action selection, will positively correlate with trial-specific drift rate estimates derived from a hierarchical Bayesian temporal discounting DDM, reflecting its role in driving the evidence accumulation process for larger-later rewards.
proposals.method: Participants will complete a temporal discounting task during fMRI. Choice and reaction time data will be fit with a hierarchical Bayesian DDM for discounting to extract trial-level posterior estimates of drift rate (v). We will use a general linear model (GLM) with trial-wise parametric modulators, where the modulator is the trial's drift rate estimate. This will identify voxels where BOLD signal co-varies with the trial-to-trial intensity of evidence accumulation. Primary region-of-interest analysis will focus on the caudate, with whole-brain exploratory analysis.
