proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct neural mapping of a trial-level DDM parameter during intertemporal choice, moving beyond correlating brain activity with overall choice or model-agnostic signals. It will establish the caudate as a key neural correlate of the evidence accumulation process in valuation, offering a more precise biomarker for studying disorders associated with altered discounting (e.g., addiction, ADHD).
proposals.novelty: While prior work has linked average discount rates or DDM parameters to neural structure/function, the trial-level analysis of a computationally pure parameter (drift rate) with fMRI is novel. This approach tests a specific mechanistic hypothesis about the caudate's role in the dynamic evidence accumulation process itself, not just outcome representation.

proposals.title: Dynamic Drift: A Reinforcement Learning-Diffusion Model of Trial-by-Trial Adaptation in Intertemporal Choice
proposals.motivation: Standard temporal discounting models treat preference as a static trait, yet real-world choices involve learning from fluctuating reward-delay contingencies. This static view fails to capture the dynamic, trial-by-trial adjustments observed in gambling and foraging contexts, limiting our understanding of impulsivity and its associated disorders. Integrating learning dynamics into discounting is crucial for a more mechanistic account of choice under temporal uncertainty.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that intertemporal choices are guided by a model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) system that dynamically updates value expectations, with these learned values driving evidence accumulation in a drift-diffusion process. This integration will better explain choice variability and reaction time patterns than static discounting or pure reinforcement learning models alone.
proposals.method: We will fit a hybrid computational model to trial-by-trial choice and reaction time data from a novel dynamic discounting task where delay-reward contingencies change probabilistically. The model will feature an MBRL agent (e.g., a successor representation or Dyna-style architecture) that learns a state transition model to predict delayed reward values. The output of this learner will parameterize the drift rate of a hierarchical drift-diffusion model (DDM), linking learned value differences to the speed and direction of evidence accumulation. Model comparison will pit this against static discounting-DDM hybrids and model-free RL-DDM models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified computational framework that formally links learning and decision processes in intertemporal choice, moving beyond trait-level discount rates. It will generate novel, testable predictions about how choice reactivity and reliability are shaped by environmental statistics, offering a more precise tool for studying impulsivity in clinical populations.
proposals.novelty: While RL and DDM have been combined for simple decisions, applying this to the temporal domain with a *model-based* learner to capture internal simulations of future states is novel. This directly tests whether dynamic discounting arises from updating a cognitive map of delay contingencies, not just reward history.

proposals.title: Dissociable Roles of the Medial Orbitofrontal Cortex in Discounting of Delay, Probability, and Effort: A Lesion Study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 neuroeconomic research implicates the medial orbitofrontal cortex (mOFC) in value-based choice, particularly in temporal discounting. However, it remains debated whether this region computes a domain-general value signal or is specifically tuned to certain dimensions of cost, like delay. Clarifying this functional specificity is critical for refining models of impulsivity and decision-making deficits observed in clinical disorders.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that mOFC lesions will cause a specific and disproportionate impairment in temporal discounting (increased preference for immediate rewards) compared to probability discounting (risk) and effort discounting (physical cost), challenging the notion of a unitary value integration role for this region.
proposals.method: We will recruit participants with focal, acquired mOFC lesions and matched controls. Using a within-subjects design, participants will complete three computerized discounting tasks (temporal, probability, effort) in a counterbalanced order. For each task, choices will be modeled using a hierarchical Bayesian implementation of the hyperbolic discounting function (for delay) and analogous hyperboloid models for probability and effort. Model parameters (discount rate k) will be estimated using Hamiltonian Monte Carlo via Stan. We will compare lesion and control groups on each discounting parameter using Bayesian linear regression, controlling for general cognitive performance.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide direct causal evidence for or against the domain-specificity of mOFC function in human discounting. It will clarify the neurocomputational architecture of cost-benefit integration and inform theoretical accounts of impulsivity by dissociating its temporal, risk-related, and effort-based components.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have associated mOFC activity with various forms of discounting, this is a direct causal test comparing three key discounting domains within the same lesion cohort, moving beyond correlational fMRI evidence to establish necessary function.

proposals.title: Dissociating Acute and Chronic Medial Orbitofrontal Contributions to Temporal Discounting: A Longitudinal Study in Progressive Neurodegeneration
proposals.motivation: The medial orbitofrontal cortex (mOFC) is strongly implicated in value-based decision-making and delay discounting, but existing cross-sectional studies cannot disentangle acute functional deficits from chronic neural reorganization. This is a critical gap, as understanding the temporal dynamics of mOFC involvement is essential for interpreting behavioral changes in both neurodegenerative disorders and acute lesions. Pre-2026, longitudinal tracking of discounting behavior alongside progressive, region-specific atrophy remains unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the rate of change in delay discounting (increased impulsivity) will correlate with the rate of mOFC atrophy over time, and that this longitudinal relationship will be a stronger predictor of behavior than cross-sectional mOFC volume, revealing a chronic, cumulative effect of mOFC degeneration distinct from acute disruption.
proposals.method: We will recruit 30 patients with behavioral variant frontotemporal dementia (bvFTD) or semantic dementia, syndromes with known progressive mOFC involvement, and 30 matched controls. Participants will complete a computerized delay discounting task and structural MRI at baseline and 12-month intervals for two years. Voxel-based morphometry will quantify mOFC atrophy rates. Discounting behavior will be modeled using hierarchical Bayesian estimation of hyperbolic (k) and drift-diffusion model (DDM) parameters (drift rate, boundary separation) to separate valuation from decision noise. Primary analysis will use linear mixed-effects models to test associations between longitudinal changes in mOFC volume and discounting parameters, controlling for general cognitive decline.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal evidence mapping the trajectory of impulsivity onto progressive mOFC loss, directly testing the chronic role of this region in temporal choice. It will establish the reliability and neural correlates of discounting measures in a clinical population over time, informing their use as potential biomarkers. The findings will clarify whether mOFC dysfunction leads to a gradual 'unraveling' of future valuation or an acute, stable deficit.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links mOFC to discounting cross-sectionally, this proposal uniquely leverages a natural longitudinal experiment (progressive neurodegeneration) to separate acute from chronic effects. Combining repeated, computationally precise behavioral modeling (DDM) with voxel-wise atrophy rates moves beyond static correlations to model dynamic brain-behavior relationships.

proposals.title: Structural Connectivity of the Medial Orbitofrontal-Hippocampal Circuit as a Neuroanatomical Predictor of Individual Differences in Delay Discounting
proposals.motivation: Delay discounting, the devaluation of future rewards, is a core process in decision-making and is associated with disorders like addiction and impulsivity. While functional studies implicate the medial orbitofrontal cortex (mOFC) and hippocampus in temporal valuation, the structural white matter pathways supporting this interaction remain poorly characterized. Establishing a direct neuroanatomical link would provide a mechanistic basis for individual differences in discounting behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that greater white matter integrity, measured via fractional anisotropy (FA), in the mOFC-hippocampal pathway will predict shallower (more patient) delay discounting rates at the individual level.
proposals.method: We will recruit 80 healthy adult participants to complete a computerized delay discounting task (e.g., adjusting-amount procedure) to derive individual discount rate (k) parameters. Each participant will undergo diffusion tensor imaging (DTI) on a 3T MRI scanner. Using FSL's FDT toolbox, we will perform probabilistic tractography seeded in the mOFC (defined via an anatomical mask) to the hippocampus to reconstruct the pathway of interest. Mean FA along this tract will be extracted and used as the primary predictor in a multiple regression model, controlling for age, sex, and whole-brain mean FA, to test its association with log-transformed discount rates.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct evidence linking the structural integrity of a specific fronto-temporal circuit to discounting behavior, moving beyond correlational functional activations. It will offer a novel neuroanatomical biomarker for individual differences in temporal choice, with implications for understanding vulnerability in disorders characterized by steep discounting.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links mOFC and hippocampal *function* to discounting, no study has specifically tested whether the *structural connectivity* between these regions predicts behavior. This bridges the gap between functional networks and their underlying anatomical architecture in temporal decision-making.

proposals.title: High-resolution fMRI Dissociation of Prospection and Valuation Signals in Medial and Lateral Orbitofrontal Cortex Subregions
proposals.motivation: The orbitofrontal cortex (OFC) is critical for value-based decision-making, but its functional topography remains debated. Pre-2026 work suggests a posterior-anterior gradient for abstract versus concrete value, and a medial-lateral distinction for reward versus punishment. However, it is unclear if separate OFC subregions specifically support the construction of prospective scenarios versus the valuation of those scenarios, a dissociation central to models of intertemporal choice and gambling where Peters' expertise lies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that medial OFC (mOFC) subregions will be preferentially engaged during the generation and elaboration of future-oriented prospects, while lateral OFC (lOFC) subregions will encode the subjective value assigned to those prospects, independent of prospect content.
proposals.method: We will use 7T fMRI with sub-millimeter resolution to scan participants (N=40) during a novel task that temporally separates prospection and valuation phases for gambles and delayed rewards. Prospection involves vividly imagining specific future outcomes, while valuation involves making binary choices. We will employ multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) to decode task phases within subregions and use a drift-diffusion model (DDM) framework, aligned with Peters' recent methods, to relate neural activity in OFC subdivisions to behavioral choice dynamics and individual differences in discounting.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first direct neural evidence for a functional dissociation between prospection and valuation within human OFC subdivisions. It will clarify the neural architecture of future-oriented decision-making and offer a mechanistic explanation for individual differences in temporal discounting and gambling, potentially linking to disorders of impulsivity.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have mapped value signals in OFC, none have used high-resolution fMRI to explicitly isolate and contrast the neural correlates of constructing a future prospect versus assigning it a value, a process-level distinction hypothesized in cognitive theory but not neurally localized.

proposals.title: A Bayesian Model Comparison of Prospection and Valuation Accounts of Medial Orbitofrontal Cortex Function in Temporal Discounting
proposals.motivation: The medial orbitofrontal cortex (mOFC) is consistently implicated in temporal discounting, but its precise computational role remains debated. Pre-2026 literature presents two dominant, non-exclusive accounts: the mOFC as a site for prospective simulation of future rewards or as a hub for integrating and comparing subjective values. Resolving this is critical for understanding disorders like addiction and impulsivity, where discounting is altered. Current studies often rely on model-agnostic neuroimaging correlations, lacking formal statistical comparison of the underlying cognitive mechanisms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that trial-by-trial neural activity patterns in the mOFC during a discounting task will be better explained by a model of prospective scene construction than by a model of value comparison. This will be formally adjudicated using Bayesian model selection at the neural level.
proposals.method: We will adapt Bayesian model selection and model-based fMRI techniques from perceptual decision-making studies. Participants will complete a virtual reality discounting task with rich, immersive future scenarios. We will construct two competing generative models: a *prospection model* that simulates future states and a *valuation model* that computes discounted values. Using hierarchical Bayesian estimation (e.g., HDDM), we will fit both models to behavioral choice data. The trial-by-trial regressors (e.g., prediction errors, prospection strength, value difference) from each winning model will then be used in a general linear model to explain mOFC BOLD activity. Formal comparison will use the Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC) and protected exceedance probabilities to determine which model's neural regressors best account for mOFC signal.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, quantitative test of competing theories of mOFC function, moving beyond association to mechanistic explanation. It will establish a novel methodological pipeline for linking cognitive models directly to neural data in value-based decision-making. The findings will clarify the core computational deficit in populations with abnormal discounting, informing more targeted interventions.
proposals.novelty: While model-based fMRI is common, applying formal Bayesian model comparison at the *neural level* to adjudicate between high-level cognitive accounts of mOFC function in discounting is novel. This approach integrates Dr. Peters' expertise in discounting, virtual reality assessment, and the mOFC with advanced computational psychiatry methods.

proposals.title: Gaze patterns as a window to prospective simulation: Eye-tracking reveals impaired prospection in medial temporal lobe lesion patients during intertemporal choice
proposals.motivation: Intertemporal choice models often assume that decision-makers prospectively simulate future outcomes, a process hypothesized to rely on the medial temporal lobe (MTL) and its role in episodic future thinking. However, direct, process-level evidence for such simulation during choice is lacking, especially in clinical populations. Eye-tracking provides a real-time, non-verbal measure of information sampling that can reveal the covert cognitive processes underlying discounting, bridging a gap between behavioral choice and neural mechanisms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that healthy participants will exhibit gaze patterns indicative of prospective simulation (e.g., prolonged fixations on delay information and dynamic saccades between reward and delay attributes), whereas patients with MTL lesions will show attenuated or altered patterns, correlating with steeper temporal discounting and reduced behavioral sensitivity to delay manipulations.
proposals.method: We will recruit patients with focal MTL lesions and matched controls to perform a computerized intertemporal choice task while eye movements are recorded. We will employ an information-board paradigm where reward magnitude and delay are presented in separate, fixatable areas. Key metrics will include dwell time proportion, fixation transitions (reward-to-delay saccades), and latency to first fixation, analyzed using mixed-effects models. We will integrate these with drift-diffusion modeling (DDM) to link gaze patterns to evidence accumulation dynamics (drift rates) and choice consistency.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct process-level evidence linking MTL function to prospection during economic choice, moving beyond correlational neuroimaging. It will validate eye-tracking as a tool for assessing cognitive deficits in clinical populations with decision-making disorders. The findings will refine theoretical models of discounting by specifying how simulation breakdowns alter the decision evidence.
proposals.novelty: While eye-tracking has been used in value-based choice, its application to probe prospective simulation in lesion patients during intertemporal decisions is novel. Combining high-temporal-resolution gaze metrics with computational DDM parameters to dissect the choice process in this population represents a significant methodological advance over standard behavioral discounting measures.

proposals.title: Maturation of the Medial Orbitofrontal Cortex and the Development of Future-Oriented Choice: A Longitudinal Neurocomputational Study
proposals.motivation: Intertemporal choice is a core component of decision-making, with steep discounting of future rewards associated with impulsivity and various disorders. The medial orbitofrontal cortex (mOFC) is strongly implicated in value representation during discounting tasks in adults, but its developmental trajectory and causal role in shaping the emergence of patient preferences in youth remain poorly understood. This gap limits our ability to model how normative neurocognitive maturation supports healthy decision-making versus pathways to maladaptive impulsivity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the structural and functional maturation of the mOFC across childhood and adolescence will be specifically associated with a decrease in the discount rate (increased patience), and that this relationship will be best captured by a drift-diffusion model framework where mOFC integrity modulates the drift rate towards larger-later choices.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal study with participants aged 8-16, assessed annually over three years. At each wave, participants will complete a validated, gamified temporal discounting task in a virtual reality environment to enhance engagement and ecological validity, with reliability of measures assessed as in Peters' prior work. Concurrent high-resolution structural (T1, T2) and resting-state fMRI will be acquired. We will model choices using hierarchical Bayesian estimation of hyperbolic (k) and quasi-hyperbolic (β, δ) discounting parameters. Neural analyses will correlate these parameters and trial-by-trial drift rates (estimated via the HDDM toolbox) with mOFC cortical thickness, volume, and functional connectivity with valuation networks (e.g., ventral striatum).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal evidence linking mOFC maturation directly to developmental changes in discounting behavior through a formal computational mechanism (drift rate). It will establish a normative neurodevelopmental trajectory for a key neural substrate of future-oriented choice, creating a benchmark against which clinical populations can be compared. The findings will inform theories of adolescent risk-taking by pinpointing a specific neural driver of increasing patience.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have correlated brain structure with discounting cross-sectionally, this proposal uniquely combines longitudinal developmental design, virtual reality assessment for reliability, and a drift-diffusion modeling approach to test how mOFC maturation shapes the evidence accumulation process underlying intertemporal decisions as they emerge.

proposals.title: Social Discounting of Future Rewards: A Drift-Diffusion Model Analysis of Intertemporal Prosocial Choice
proposals.motivation: Temporal discounting research has robustly characterized how individuals devalue personal future rewards, linking steep discounting to disorders like addiction and impulsivity. However, real-world decisions often involve trading off immediate personal costs for delayed benefits to others (e.g., charity, environmental action). Pre-2026 work lacks a rigorous, mechanistic model of how delay and social distance interact in such social intertemporal choices, limiting our understanding of prosocial behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in social intertemporal choices, discount rates will be lower (more patient) for socially close others compared to strangers, but that this social discounting effect will be attenuated as delay increases, due to increased noise or value uncertainty in the decision process.
proposals.method: We will adapt a validated virtual reality temporal discounting task, replacing self-reward with rewards for a specified beneficiary (e.g., friend, stranger). Participants will make binary choices between a smaller immediate reward for the beneficiary and a larger delayed one. We will employ a hierarchical Bayesian drift-diffusion model (DDM) to analyze choices and reaction times. The DDM will parameterize drift rate as a function of the subjective value difference (incorporating both delay and social distance) and boundary separation, allowing us to dissociate effects on value evaluation from decision caution. We will also assess test-retest reliability and physiological reactivity (e.g., skin conductance) during decisions.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first formal computational model of social intertemporal choice, quantifying how social and temporal distances jointly influence decision dynamics. It will extend discounting theory beyond the individual and establish a reliable paradigm for studying disorders characterized by social dysfunction (e.g., antisocial personality disorder) where social future planning may be impaired.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied social and temporal discounting separately, this integrates them within a single, mechanistic DDM framework to reveal the cognitive process underlying their interaction, moving beyond descriptive discount functions to a process model of choice.

proposals.title: Single-Unit Activity in the Medial Orbitofrontal Cortex Encodes Subjective Value During Intertemporal Choice in Humans
proposals.motivation: Human neuroimaging has robustly linked the medial orbitofrontal cortex (mOFC) and ventromedial prefrontal cortex to the computation of subjective value during discounting. However, the BOLD signal aggregates activity across millions of neurons and hundreds of milliseconds, obscuring the precise neural coding mechanisms. Understanding how individual neurons represent immediate and delayed rewards is critical for linking neural dynamics to behavioral phenomena like impulsivity and its associated disorders.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that single neurons in the human mOFC will exhibit firing rates that scale with the subjective value of offered rewards, irrespective of delay, and that the population dynamics of these value-encoding neurons will predict trial-by-trial choice variability and impulsivity.
proposals.method: We will record single-unit and multi-unit activity from depth electrodes implanted in the mOFC of neurosurgical patients (e.g., with epilepsy) performing a computerized intertemporal choice task. Patients will make a series of choices between smaller-sooner and larger-later monetary rewards. Neural data will be aligned to offer presentation and analyzed using established pre-2026 methods: peri-stimulus time histograms, generalized linear models to regress firing rates against subjective value (calculated via individually fitted hyperbolic discounting models), and drift-diffusion modeling to link neural firing patterns to decision dynamics and behavioral reactivity.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct evidence of subjective value coding at the single-neuron level in the human mOFC during intertemporal choice. It will bridge the gap between primate single-unit studies and human fMRI, offering a mechanistic explanation for how value signals are integrated during deliberation. The findings will establish a micro-scale neural correlate for discounting behavior, with implications for understanding the neural basis of pathological impulsivity in gambling and other disorders.
proposals.novelty: While single-neuron recordings during reward processing exist in primates and in human hippocampus during memory tasks, applying this technique to directly capture value computation during discounting in the human mOFC is novel. It moves beyond correlational BOLD signals to test a mechanistic coding hypothesis at the cellular level in humans.

proposals.title: Modeling the Impact of Sleep Deprivation on Value-Based Choice: A Reinforcement Learning Analysis of Learning Rate and Choice Stochasticity
proposals.motivation: Sleep deprivation robustly impairs decision-making, increasing impulsivity and risk-taking, but the precise computational mechanisms remain unclear. Pre-2026 research on temporal discounting and gambling under fatigue has largely relied on descriptive models, failing to capture the trial-by-trial dynamics of value learning. Integrating reinforcement learning (RL) with sleep science offers a mechanistic account of how fatigue alters the core processes of reward integration and action selection.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that sleep deprivation reduces the learning rate in a reinforcement learning model, reflecting impaired value updating, and simultaneously increases the inverse temperature parameter, indicating more stochastic and impulsive choices.
proposals.method: We will recruit participants for a multi-session study involving a sleep-deprived and a rested condition. Participants will complete a probabilistic reversal learning task and a sequential decision-making task with variable rewards. We will fit choice data using a Q-learning model and a more sophisticated Actor-Critic framework. Model comparison will focus on parameters for learning rate (alpha) and choice stochasticity (beta), assessing their modulation by sleep condition and individual differences in baseline impulsivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal computational account of sleep-deprived choice, moving beyond correlational findings to identify specific, altered RL parameters. It will bridge Peters' expertise in discounting and gambling with modern RL methods, offering a novel framework for understanding disorders of impulsivity and cognitive control associated with sleep disruption.
proposals.novelty: While RL models are established, their application to sleep deprivation to dissociate effects on learning rate from choice noise is novel. This goes beyond prior work that primarily links fatigue to increased discounting or simple performance deficits, instead targeting the dynamic learning process itself.

proposals.title: Enhancing Sleep to Amplify Neural Value Signals and Reduce Impulsive Choice
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research robustly links sleep deprivation to impaired decision-making, including steeper temporal discounting and reduced neural sensitivity to reward value. However, this work primarily establishes a deficit model. It remains untested whether proactively enhancing sleep quality—beyond baseline restoration—can produce the inverse effect: amplifying the neural signals that support valuation and self-control, thereby improving choice. This shifts the focus from mitigating impairment to optimizing function.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-component sleep enhancement intervention will increase the amplitude of parametric value signals in the ventromedial prefrontal cortex (vmPFC) and reduce conflict-related signals in the anterior cingulate cortex (ACC) during a temporal discounting task, leading to more patient, less impulsive choices.
proposals.method: We will recruit 120 participants and use a randomized controlled design with an active sleep enhancement group (cognitive-behavioral therapy for insomnia, sleep hygiene, and sleep extension) versus a placebo sleep hygiene control. Pre- and post-intervention, participants will complete a monetary discounting task during fMRI. We will apply a hierarchical drift-diffusion model (HDDM) to choice data to isolate drift rate (evidence accumulation speed) and boundary separation (response caution). Neural analyses will use general linear models to identify parametric BOLD responses to offer value in vmPFC and conflict signals in ACC, testing for group-by-time interactions.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first causal evidence that sleep enhancement can strengthen core neural computations for value-based choice, moving beyond correlational sleep-quality studies. It will directly link improved sleep to quantifiable changes in the drift-diffusion parameters of decision-making, offering a mechanistic account for reduced impulsivity.
proposals.novelty: While sleep loss is known to harm decision-making, testing the 'inverse problem'—whether enhanced sleep can boost valuation signals—is a novel, proactive approach. Combining fMRI with computational modeling of choice (drift diffusion) to quantify intervention effects on decision dynamics represents a methodological advance over standard behavioral assays.

proposals.title: Enhancing Episodic Future Thinking in Temporal Discounting: A Virtual Reality Intervention to Reduce Impulsivity
proposals.motivation: Episodic future thinking (EFT) interventions can reduce delay discounting, a core process in impulsivity associated with disorders like addiction and gambling. However, standard EFT relies on verbal cues, which may lack the vividness needed for robust effects. Pre-2026 research shows VR's efficacy in clinical exposure therapy, suggesting its potential to create more immersive and affectively engaging future simulations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an immersive VR-based EFT intervention will produce more vivid future simulations and a greater reduction in delay discounting rates compared to a standard (verbal cue) EFT condition and a control condition.
proposals.method: We will adapt VR environments from pre-2026 clinical psychology studies (e.g., for PTSD) to create personalized, positive future event scenarios (e.g., a graduation). Participants will complete a temporal discounting task (using a drift-diffusion model analysis to isolate decision components) in three within-subjects conditions: VR-EFT, standard EFT, and a neutral control. Vividness and emotional reactivity will be measured via self-report and physiological indices (e.g., skin conductance).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel methodological advance for EFT research by quantitatively testing VR's efficacy. It will clarify the mechanism by linking simulation vividness to discounting behavior and neural reactivity patterns, potentially informing more effective interventions for impulse control disorders.
proposals.novelty: While VR has been used to assess discounting reliability, its application as an active intervention tool to *enhance* EFT vividness is novel. This bridges clinical VR methods with cognitive models of future thinking and decision-making.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Drift: Quantifying the Impact of Gambling Policy Changes on Population-Level Temporal Discounting
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research robustly links individual differences in temporal discounting to gambling disorder severity and reactivity to near rewards. However, it remains unknown whether macro-level policy environments, such as the introduction of new gambling venues or advertising restrictions, can causally shift the discounting distributions of entire populations over time. This gap limits our ability to design policies that mitigate the psychological drivers of harmful gambling at a public health scale.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that policy changes that increase gambling accessibility (e.g., casino openings) will be associated with a population-level increase in discount rates (greater impulsivity), while restrictive policies (e.g., advertising bans) will be associated with a decrease.
proposals.method: We will implement a large-scale, longitudinal virtual reality (VR) study, leveraging the reliability of VR-based discounting measures established in prior work. We will recruit a demographically stratified panel of participants (N~5000) from regions undergoing planned policy changes. Participants will complete a drift diffusion modeling (DDM)-adapted temporal discounting task in VR at multiple timepoints (pre-policy, 6-months, 12-months post-change). We will use hierarchical Bayesian modeling to estimate population- and individual-level discounting parameters (k) and DDM drift rates, testing for systematic shifts associated with policy type while controlling for confounds.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first causal evidence for how regulatory environments shape core decision-making psychology at the population level. It will deliver a novel methodological framework for policy evaluation based on computational cognitive metrics, moving beyond traditional economic or prevalence measures. The findings will directly inform public health strategies aimed at reducing gambling-related harm by targeting its cognitive antecedents.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies associate discounting with individual gambling, this project innovates by testing policy as a causal, ecological driver of population-level psychological change, integrating DDM with large-scale longitudinal VR assessment in a natural experiment design.

proposals.title: Hyperbolic Discounting of Future Losses: A Computational Comparison of Gambling Disorder and Healthy Controls
proposals.motivation: Temporal discounting of rewards is a well-established transdiagnostic marker in addiction, including gambling disorder. However, the 'dual problem'—how individuals discount future losses and debts—is critically understudied, despite its direct relevance to the accrual of gambling-related financial harm. Understanding if and how gamblers devalue future negative consequences could inform models of persistence in maladaptive behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: Individuals with gambling disorder will exhibit shallower discounting of future losses (i.e., treat them as less aversive) compared to healthy controls, and this pattern will be associated with clinical measures of disorder severity and financial reactivity.
proposals.method: We will recruit 50 participants with gambling disorder and 50 matched controls. Participants will complete a validated temporal discounting task for losses (e.g., choosing between a smaller immediate loss and a larger delayed loss). We will fit choice data using the hyperbolic discounting model (k parameter) and the drift-diffusion model (DDM) to separate discounting rates from decision noise and evidence accumulation drift rates. Clinical assessments of gambling disorder severity and a reactivity scale measuring emotional response to gambling debts will be administered.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct computational comparison of loss discounting between gamblers and controls, quantifying both discount rates and underlying decision processes. It will establish whether attenuated sensitivity to future losses is a core computational phenotype in gambling disorder, offering a novel target for interventions aimed at reducing harm.
proposals.novelty: While discounting of rewards is extensively mapped, the systematic examination of loss discounting in gambling disorder using joint computational modeling (hyperbolic + DDM) is novel. It tests the specific hypothesis that the disorder is characterized by a dual deficit: steep reward discounting *and* shallow loss discounting.

proposals.title: Dissociating the Role of Frontal Pole from Subcortical Systems in Uncertainty-Driven Exploration During Temporal Discounting and Gambling Tasks
proposals.motivation: Prominent models of decision-making, grounded in pre-2026 primate and human fMRI studies, posit the frontal pole (FP) as a critical hub for directing exploration under uncertainty. However, recent null findings from our lab challenge this centrality, suggesting a potential overestimation of its role. Resolving this discrepancy is crucial for building accurate neural models of choice, particularly for disorders like addiction and ADHD where exploration/exploitation imbalances are associated with maladaptive temporal discounting and gambling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that uncertainty-driven exploration in value-based choice is not critically dependent on the frontal pole but is instead primarily mediated by subcortical systems (e.g., ventral striatum, amygdala) and their interactions with other prefrontal regions (e.g., anterior cingulate cortex).
proposals.method: We will recruit 120 participants to perform a novel multi-armed bandit task embedded within a virtual reality (VR) environment, integrating gambling and intertemporal choice elements. Neural activity will be recorded using high-density EEG to capture the temporal dynamics of exploration. We will fit participant choices to a hierarchical drift-diffusion model (HDDM) with an explicit uncertainty bonus parameter. Trial-by-trial estimates of exploration will be used to conduct EEG source localization and connectivity analyses (using pre-2026 methods like dynamic causal modeling or Granger causality) to test for reactivity in FP versus subcortical sources.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a rigorous test of the FP's necessity in exploration, potentially redirecting theoretical focus toward subcortical-prefrontal circuits. It will also establish the reliability of neural correlates of exploration by combining computational modeling with VR-embedded tasks and high-temporal-resolution neuroimaging.
proposals.novelty: The proposal directly tests a null finding against a dominant theory using a methodologically integrated approach (VR, HDDM, EEG connectivity) not commonly applied to this specific question, moving beyond simple fMRI localization to dissect the temporal dynamics and network interactions underlying exploratory choice.

proposals.title: Strategic Perseveration as Adaptive Threshold Modulation in a Drift-Diffusion Model of Explore-Exploit Choice
proposals.motivation: In temporal and risky choice, perseveration is often treated as a maladaptive bias or noise. However, recent theories suggest it may be a strategic adaptation to environmental statistics. Current drift-diffusion models (DDMs) of value-based choice use static thresholds, failing to capture how decision-makers dynamically adjust their persistence based on inferred option stability, a critical component of real-world explore-exploit dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: Strategic perseveration in sequential choice is governed by an adaptive decision threshold within a DDM, where the threshold dynamically widens in response to accumulating evidence for a chosen option's value stability, optimally balancing exploitation and exploration.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical Bayesian DDM where the decision threshold is a function of a latent volatility estimate, updated trial-by-trial via a Bayesian belief updating mechanism (e.g., a hidden Markov model). The model will be fit to choice and reaction time data from a novel multi-armed bandit task with embedded volatile and stable phases, administered to participants in a controlled lab and a virtual reality environment to assess reliability. Model comparison will pit this adaptive-threshold DDM against static-threshold DDMs and reinforcement learning models with choice stickiness.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, mechanistic account of strategic perseveration, linking computational psychiatry concepts of adaptive perseveration to core decision neuroscience methods. It will yield a novel, quantifiable measure of threshold reactivity as a potential individual difference marker relevant to disorders of impulsivity and compulsivity.
proposals.novelty: While DDMs are standard for two-alternative choice, integrating a dynamically modulated threshold driven by a latent volatility signal to explain perseverative runs in explore-exploit tasks is a significant theoretical and computational advance beyond static bias terms.

proposals.title: Modeling the Dynamics of Explore-Exploit Decisions: A Drift Diffusion Framework for Temporal Gambling Tasks
proposals.motivation: Explore-exploit decisions are fundamental to adaptive behavior but are often modeled with static, summary statistics like discount rates, which obscure the moment-to-moment cognitive dynamics. Pre-2026 work in perceptual decision-making has successfully used sequential sampling models, like the drift diffusion model (DDM), to decompose choices into evidence accumulation rates and decision thresholds. Applying this dynamic framework to value-based explore-exploit choices could reveal novel computational mechanisms underlying impulsivity and suboptimal foraging.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the dynamics of explore-exploit choices, particularly in temporal gambling paradigms, are best captured by a DDM where the drift rate is modulated by the evolving subjective value difference between options, and that individual differences in discounting are associated with specific DDM parameters like drift rate and boundary separation.
proposals.method: We will adapt a multi-armed bandit task with temporally delayed rewards, administered in a virtual reality environment to enhance ecological validity and participant engagement. Trial-by-trial choice and reaction time data will be fit using hierarchical Bayesian implementations of the DDM (e.g., HDDM). The core model will feature a drift rate determined by the difference in temporally discounted values of the exploit (known) and explore (uncertain) options, with discounting parameters estimated concurrently. We will test for associations between DDM parameters (drift, boundary, non-decision time) and clinical measures of impulsivity and reactivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a process-level, mechanistic account of explore-exploit trade-offs, moving beyond descriptive discount functions. It will establish a novel link between the computational neuroscience of perceptual decisions and value-based choice, offering more precise targets for understanding disorders characterized by impaired decision-making, such as addiction and ADHD.
proposals.novelty: While DDM has been applied to simple intertemporal choices, its application to the dynamic, information-seeking context of explore-exploit dilemmas with temporal uncertainty is novel. The integration of trial-by-trial value computation directly into the drift process of a sequential sampling model for this problem space is a significant methodological advance.

ideas.description: This research direction integrates computational reinforcement learning (RL) modeling with genetic fine-mapping to test whether DRD2 expression is the sole mechanistic driver of reward-related phenotypes. We will apply RL models to decompose behavioral and fMRI data from individuals with known genetic variation at the DRD2 locus into computationally pure components (e.g., prediction error, expected value). By then examining associations between these component signals and the expression of DRD2 versus other genes in the locus (using cis-eQTL data), we can determine if genetic effects are specific to prediction error (suggesting a primary DRD2 role) or are distributed across multiple RL computations, implicating a broader polygenic mechanism within the locus.

ideas.title: Drift diffusion modeling of genetic susceptibility to peer influence on smoking initiation
ideas.description: This research fuses genetic epidemiology with cognitive computational modeling to investigate how TTC12-ANKK1-DRD2 risk variants modulate the temporal dynamics of explore-exploit decisions in social contexts. We will apply drift diffusion models to behavioral choice data from tasks where participants can explore novel activities or exploit known rewards in environments with simulated peer smoking cues. This allows us to test whether genetic risk alters core decision parameters—such as drift rate (sensitivity to peer evidence) or decision threshold—when evaluating smoking initiation opportunities. The approach reveals not just if, but how these gene-environment interactions amplify risk by distorting the real-time cognitive process of social learning and valuation.

ideas.title: Dopaminergic precision as a neurocomputational biomarker for adolescent smoking disclosure bias
ideas.description: This research direction proposes that the social desirability bias affecting adolescent smoking self-report is not merely a social-cognitive artifact but is implemented by dopaminergic modulation of decision precision during the disclosure decision. We hypothesize that when an adolescent considers disclosing smoking, the dopaminergic signal encodes the precision (inverse decision noise) of the value comparison between truth-telling and social conformity, with lower precision leading to more stochastic and unreliable reports. By measuring this dopaminergic precision signal (e.g., via pupillometry, computational modeling of choice variability, or neuroimaging), we can derive an objective, trial-by-trial biomarker for the reliability of a given self-reported answer, fundamentally reframing self-report data from a binary truth/falsehood to a probability distribution weighted by the brain's internal confidence in its own disclosure.

ideas.title: Strategic Perseveration as a Drift Diffusion Process in Explore-Exploit Dynamics
ideas.description: This research synthesizes the adaptive perseveration and drift-diffusion modeling ideas to propose that strategic persistence is governed by a dynamic decision threshold within a sequential sampling framework. Instead of modeling perseveration as a static bias or nuisance, it is recast as the outcome of an adaptive drift process that accumulates evidence for the relative stability or volatility of the current option's value. This allows the model to quantitatively predict the optimal timing of perseverative runs and exploratory switches based on latent decision variables like decision confidence and environmental uncertainty. The result is a unified theory that explains both the cognitive mechanism and the strategic rationale of perseveration in temporal choice.

ideas.description: This study integrates ecological momentary assessment (EMA) with a randomized micro-intervention design to test the causal role of reward experiences in smoking urges. Genotyped adolescents use a smartphone app that not only assesses real-time reward and urges but also randomly assigns brief, in-the-moment activities to experimentally elevate reward states. By comparing urge levels following reward-boosting versus control prompts within individuals, the design establishes a causal link between momentary reward and smoking motivation. This approach directly tests whether specific genetic variants moderate an individual's susceptibility to urges when reward is low and their responsiveness to behavioral reward enhancement.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Multi-Attribute Reinforcement Learning for Decomposing Dynamic Reward Integration
ideas.description: This research applies a hierarchical, multi-attribute computational reinforcement learning framework to decompose neural reward signals. The model separately tracks and learns predictions for distinct reward attributes (e.g., magnitude, probability, delay) using parallel, interacting learning systems. A higher-level meta-controller dynamically weights the contribution of each attribute stream to a unified value representation and policy selection based on contextual reliability and volatility. This approach allows for the precise decomposition of neural activity into prediction errors and values specific to individual reward dimensions and their context-dependent integration.

ideas.description: This project integrates a multi-agent bandit task, where human participants make exploratory choices while observing others, with a genetically-informed, single-cell transcriptomic analysis of post-mortem striatal tissue. Participants are genotyped for the DRD2-linked variant rs2236709, and their social exploration strategies are quantified. Subsequently, single-nucleus RNA sequencing is performed on donor striatal tissue, stratified by genotype, to identify which specific striatal cell populations (e.g., D1- vs. D2-medium spiny neurons, interneurons) show DRD2 expression differences associated with the variant. The synergy lies in correlating these cell-type-specific molecular signatures with the precise behavioral phenotypes of social information use, moving beyond association to propose a mechanistic circuit model for how genetic variation in dopamine signaling shapes social exploration.

ideas.title: Perseveration as a Strategic Exploration Tax
ideas.description: This idea reframes the perseveration component in cognitive models as an adaptive exploration-limiting strategy rather than a modeling nuisance term. Drawing an analogy from Evolutionary Ecology and Foraging Theory, where animals optimally balance exploration and exploitation through strategies like area-restricted search, we adapt this solution. The analogous solution treats behavioral 'stickiness' as a rational response to environmental costs, not a failure. The adapted research idea models perseveration as a strategic 'exploration tax' or inertia, where an agent rationally maintains its current action until the estimated switching costs outweigh the potential benefits of exploring a new option. This transforms the perseveration parameter from a noise term into a core, tunable mechanism implementing a cost-sensitive explore-exploit policy.

ideas.title: Longitudinal tracking of rs2236709 carriers with ecological momentary assessment and predictive modeling
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from meteorology and climate science, where high-frequency sensor data is used to model trajectories and predict long-term outcomes. The analogous problem of predicting climate states from initial atmospheric conditions is solved by collecting dense, real-time data and using dynamical models to identify critical transition points. Adapted to the original idea, the study would follow rs2236709 carriers longitudinally from adolescence, but employ Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) via smartphones/wearables to capture real-time reward sensitivity, mood, and craving data. This rich dataset would be analyzed with advanced time-series and predictive algorithms to model the developmental trajectory, identifying early behavioral signatures and tipping points that forecast the severity of adult nicotine dependence, thereby testing whether adolescent reward sensitivity patterns are predictive.

ideas.title: Causal, Multi-Modal Bayesian Inference of Neuromodulatory States from Dissociated Behavioral Computations
ideas.description: This adversarially improved approach reframes the inverse problem as a Bayesian causal inference challenge. It employs a hierarchical modeling framework where behavior on a battery of specially designed decision-making tasks generates posterior distributions over latent computational parameters. These parameters are then linked to underlying neurobiology through a learned generative model, which is informed and constrained by direct, though sparse, neurochemical measurements from a calibration cohort. The final output is not a point estimate of 'dopamine tone,' but a probabilistically grounded profile of likely neuromodulatory states with quantified uncertainty, robust to the ill-posedness of the original formulation.

ideas.title: Cell-type-resolved causal mediation of dopamine signaling from genotype to exploration
ideas.description: This project integrates single-cell transcriptomic profiling of a human genetic variant (rs2236709) affecting DRD2 expression with causal mediation analysis in human neuroimaging. We first use snRNA-seq on post-mortem striatal tissue to identify the specific cell type(s) where the variant alters DRD2 expression. We then recruit human carriers and non-carriers of the variant for a behavioral exploration task during fMRI, with pharmacological manipulation of dopamine signaling. The causal mediation analysis formally tests a multi-level pathway: genetic variant -> cell-type-specific DRD2 expression -> dopamine-modulated insula activity -> exploratory behavior, moving beyond association to a mechanistic, cell-type-informed causal model.

ideas.title: Inferring Dopaminergic Tone via Behavioral Deconvolution
ideas.description: This idea inverts the causal direction to infer individual dopaminergic tone from exploration-exploitation behavior alone, drawing an analogy to geophysical inverse problems. In seismology, subsurface earth structure is inferred by deconvolving observed seismic surface waves using models of wave propagation. The adapted solution treats observed behavioral choice sequences as the output signal. A pre-established, generative computational model (e.g., a reinforcement learning agent with a tunable dopamine-like parameter) serves as the forward propagation model. By running an inverse optimization (e.g., maximum likelihood estimation or Bayesian inversion) on an individual's behavioral data against this model, we can deconvolve or 'solve for' the latent dopaminergic tone parameters that most likely generated that specific behavioral signature, providing a non-invasive assay of neuromodulatory state.

ideas.title: Signal Decomposition of Reward Anticipation Using Blind Source Separation from Audio Engineering
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogical solution from audio signal processing to computational neuroscience. In audio engineering, Blind Source Separation techniques like Independent Component Analysis are used to decompose a mixed audio signal into its constituent source signals. Adapting this, we propose treating measured neural reward anticipation signals (e.g., BOLD response in fMRI or oscillatory power in EEG) as a linear mixture of underlying computational variables from reinforcement learning theory, such as prediction error and expected value. The adapted method would use BSS/ICA to statistically isolate these components based on their independence and spatiotemporal characteristics, providing a model-agnostic decomposition that complements and validates traditional model-based regression approaches.

ideas.title: Implicit Uncertainty via Learning Dynamics for Directed Model-Free Exploration
ideas.description: This idea overcomes the inefficiency of purely heuristic exploration by using the agent's own learning process as an implicit guide. It trains a lightweight module that monitors prediction error reduction or gradient magnitudes for different experiences, creating a saliency map of where the agent is currently learning the most. This 'learning progress' signal is then used as an intrinsic reward or exploration bonus, driving the agent to seek out regions where its models are improving rapidly. The approach remains model-free in the traditional sense—it does not maintain explicit probabilistic uncertainty—but achieves directed, uncertainty-like exploration by meta-learning over its own internal learning dynamics.

ideas.title: Model-free exploration heuristics to dissect polygenic contributions in behavioral genetics
ideas.description: This research direction challenges two foundational assumptions simultaneously: that optimal exploration requires explicit uncertainty representation, and that behavioral effects of a genetic locus can be attributed to a single candidate gene like DRD2. We propose using model-free exploration heuristics (e.g., novelty-seeking, directed tinkering) as behavioral probes in genetically engineered animal models. By observing how these heuristics are altered by manipulating the entire locus versus DRD2 alone, we can infer the polygenic contributions of co-regulated genes without relying on complex Bayesian computational models of the brain. This creates a powerful, parsimonious experimental loop where simple behavioral algorithms reveal the complexity of genetic mechanisms.

ideas.title: Instrumental Variable Analysis for Causal Inference of Economic Shocks on Conflict
ideas.description: This idea expands the original Mendelian randomization concept by drawing a direct analogy from instrumental variable methods in econometrics, used to establish causality between economic shocks and social outcomes like conflict. In that field, exogenous instruments (e.g., rainfall variation) are used to circumvent confounding. We adapt this solution by formally treating the genetic variant rs2236709 as an instrumental variable for the latent trait of reward sensitivity. The adapted research idea involves applying the full econometric IV framework—including tests for instrument strength (F-statistic), exclusion restriction, and monotonicity—to estimate the local average causal effect of reward sensitivity on smoking initiation and persistence, thereby strengthening the causal inference beyond standard MR association tests.

ideas.title: Sex-stratified dopaminergic exploration: Lessons from cardiovascular medicine's inclusion revolution
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from cardiovascular medicine's decades-long correction of male-biased research. Heart disease research historically excluded women, producing diagnostic criteria and treatments optimized for male physiology. The correction required not just including women but fundamentally rethinking endpoints, biomarkers, and treatment protocols. Similarly, dopamine exploration studies have relied predominantly on male participants, risking a male-normative model of exploratory behavior. The adapted approach would: (1) recruit balanced sex cohorts while tracking menstrual cycle phase and hormonal contraceptive use, mirroring cardiology's attention to hormonal context; (2) employ pharmacological challenges (e.g., L-DOPA, haloperidol) with sex-stratified dose-response analyses, analogous to how cardiology discovered sex-differential drug responses; (3) develop separate computational models for male and female exploration parameters rather than fitting both sexes to a single model, paralleling the development of sex-specific cardiac risk calculators; and (4) use neuroimaging to test whether striatal dopamine signaling during exploration-exploitation decisions shows qualitatively different network engagement patterns between sexes, as cardiac imaging revealed distinct female-pattern ischemia. This framework would determine whether sex differences in dopaminergic exploration are quantitative (same mechanism, different magnitude) or qualitative (distinct neural substrates), with direct implications for personalized approaches to disorders involving exploration deficits, such as depression and addiction, which also show marked sex differences in prevalence and treatment response.

ideas.title: Integrating Core System Models with Population-Wide Data for Improved Prediction of Adolescent Smoking
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from climate science, where detailed core models are fused with broad observational data. In climate prediction, a physics-based dynamical core is combined with vast data assimilation to improve forecasts. Here, we treat variants in the TTC12-ANKK1-DRD2 dopaminergic pathway—a well-characterized 'mechanistic core' for nicotine response—analogously to that dynamical core. We then integrate this core model with genome-wide loci from large adolescent cohorts using a hierarchical Bayesian framework. This framework uses the core pathway to set informed prior probabilities for the effects of GWAS-derived variants, allowing the population-wide data to refine the prediction while being constrained by established neurobiology. The result is a novel polygenic score that improves the prediction of adolescent smoking initiation and progression by synergistically combining deep, focused biological insight with broad, data-driven patterns.

ideas.title: Strategic Social Inference in Competitive Multi-Agent Bandit Games
ideas.description: This research investigates social exploration in settings where agents have competing objectives and limited visibility into others' rewards. Participants must explore bandit options while observing a subset of others' choices, requiring them to infer the hidden reward functions and exploration strategies of their competitors to guide their own actions. The design systematically varies the alignment of agent goals and the observability of action-outcome pairs to dissect the computational strategies underlying strategic social learning. This approach tests whether agents develop sophisticated models of others' minds or fall back on simpler heuristics like imitation when exploration has a competitive social cost.

ideas.title: Hybrid Active Inference for Scalable Exploration via Amortized Posterior and Particle-Based Planning
ideas.description: This research develops a scalable, practical framework for exploration by integrating the normative principles of active inference with modern deep reinforcement learning techniques. It overcomes the core intractability of exact variational inference by using deep neural networks to amortize the computation of posteriors over states and policies, and employs particle-based planning to approximate expected free energy. The resulting agent can perform uncertainty-driven exploration in high-dimensional, partially observable environments while maintaining a direct link to the theoretical constructs of epistemic value and dopaminergic signaling. This enables both theoretical unification and empirical validation on benchmark tasks where pure active inference methods fail.

ideas.title: Dynamic Interaction-Weighted Polygenic Scores for Adolescent Smoking Onset
ideas.description: This study constructs a time-sensitive polygenic risk score that models how genetic susceptibility interacts with time-varying environmental exposures to predict smoking initiation in adolescence. Instead of a static genetic score, we develop an interaction-weighted PRS (iwPRS) where the contribution of genetic variants is dynamically scaled by longitudinally assessed environmental contexts, including peer networks, family conflict, and school environment, measured at multiple time points. The model is trained using data from cohorts with deep phenotypic longitudinal tracking to capture developmental windows of heightened GxE effect. This approach aims to substantially improve predictive accuracy by directly modeling the conditional nature of genetic risk expression.

A statistical model treats the true smoking behavior as a latent variable informed by these convergent measures, accounting for the error structure and bias of each method. This produces a probabilistically estimated phenotype that is far more reliable for etiological research than any single measure.

ideas.title: A Stress-Corrosion Cracking Model for Gene-Environment Interaction in Smoking Initiation
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from materials science's stress-corrosion cracking model, this research expands the original gene-environment interaction idea. In engineering, catastrophic failure requires a susceptible material under both a corrosive agent and mechanical stress. Adapted here, we hypothesize that smoking initiation 'failure' requires the susceptible TTC12-ANKK1-DRD2 genetic background, the corrosive social environment of peer smoking, AND an applied psychosocial 'stress' (e.g., life stress, trait impulsivity). The study would employ a longitudinal cohort design to test this three-way interaction, measuring genetic risk, time-varying peer smoking exposure, and individual stress/impulsivity metrics to see if the genetic effect is specifically amplified under the confluence of both environmental risk factors, thereby identifying a critical, high-risk context.

ideas.title: Exploration via Thermodynamic Sampling Heuristics
ideas.description: This idea challenges the Bayesian assumption that explicit uncertainty representation is necessary for efficient exploration. It draws an analogy from statistical physics, where particles explore complex energy landscapes through temperature-driven stochastic processes like Brownian motion and simulated annealing, without any internal model of the landscape's uncertainty. The adapted research idea is to design model-free exploration heuristics for RL agents that use a 'temperature' parameter governing action randomness, combined with an annealing schedule that systematically reduces this temperature over time. This creates a exploration-to-exploitation transition driven purely by a simple, tunable stochastic process, requiring no computation of posteriors, confidence intervals, or other explicit uncertainty metrics.

ideas.title: Leveraging Population-Specific Reference Panels to Map Ancestry-Diverse Genetic Associations
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from comparative linguistics, where language evolution is studied using family-specific trees rather than a single reference language, this idea adapts the solution to genetic association studies. The analogous problem of avoiding bias from a single reference is solved in linguistics by building detailed comparisons within each language family. Adapted to genetics, the research idea is to leverage population-specific reference panels (e.g., from TOPMed, AABC, or other diverse biobanks) for major non-European ancestries. For each population, researchers would first fine-map the genomic region containing rs2236709 to account for unique linkage disequilibrium patterns and identify the likely causal variant(s). Then, they would test the association of these population-specific candidate variants with both smoking behavior and intermediate phenotypes of the reward pathway, moving beyond simple replication of the European signal to discover ancestry-specific genetic architectures.

ideas.title: Causal dissection of rs2236709 on striatal DRD2 regulation via integrated human tissue profiling and isogenic hPSC models
ideas.description: This project first performs single-nucleus RNA sequencing on post-mortem human striatal tissue from donors genotyped for rs2236709 to map cell-type-specific DRD2 expression associations in a native context. To directly test causality, it employs an isogenic human pluripotent stem cell (hPSC) system where the rs2236709 allele is edited via CRISPR-Cas9, creating genetically identical lines that differ only at this locus. These isogenic lines are differentiated into striatal medium spiny neurons (MSNs), and single-cell RNA-seq is performed to isolate the cell-intrinsic, direct transcriptional effect of the variant on DRD2, controlling for confounding genetic and environmental factors. The integrated analysis distinguishes primary regulatory consequences from secondary, tissue-level adaptations, providing a mechanistic link between genotype and cell-type-specific gene expression.

ideas.title: Orthogonal genetic perturbations to isolate DRD2 sufficiency and necessity within a co-regulated locus
ideas.description: This study directly tests whether DRD2 upregulation is sufficient to recapitulate phenotypic effects, and necessary for effects arising from locus-wide upregulation, by decoupling its expression from neighboring genes. Using CRISPRa, DRD2 expression will be selectively increased without altering co-regulated genes in the locus, testing sufficiency. Conversely, in cellular or animal models where the entire risk locus is activated, DRD2-specific CRISPRi or pharmacological antagonism will be applied to test if blocking DRD2 alone abolishes the phenotype, establishing necessity. This paired, orthogonal approach moves beyond correlation to establish direct causal links for DRD2 within its genomic context.

ideas.title: Gene Locus Deconvolution via Cis-Regulatory Module Analysis
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that DRD2 upregulation alone mechanistically drives an observed effect by proposing that other genes in the locus, co-regulated through shared cis-regulatory elements, may contribute. Drawing an analogy from Evolutionary Developmental Biology, where the role of single Hox genes was deconvoluted by analyzing conserved cis-regulatory modules controlling gene clusters, we adapt this solution. The adapted research involves mapping the shared enhancers and 3D chromatin architecture of the DRD2 locus to define its cis-regulatory module, then using targeted epigenetic perturbations to dissect the individual and synergistic contributions of all genes within this co-regulated unit to the final phenotype, moving beyond a single-gene mediator model.
